title: New MODBEACON RAT Uses gRPC Streaming for Encrypted C2 Traffic
blurb: China-linked Silver Fox is deploying MODBEACON, a Rust-based modular RAT that reuses Xray/V2Ray gRPC tunneling for encrypted C2.

title: Google's TabFM skips per-dataset training and still predicts on tables it's never seen
blurb: Google Research introduced TabFM, a foundation model that treats tabular prediction as in-context learning and generates predictions for an unseen table in a single forward pass. The approach skips the per-dataset training, hyperparameter tuning, and feature engineering that gradient-boosted trees like XGBoost require, aiming to cut time-to-production from weeks of pipeline work to one API call.

title: GhostLock, a stack-UAF that has existed in ALL Linux distributions for 15 years
blurb: Researchers at VEGA disclosed GhostLock (CVE-2026-43499), a use-after-free bug in the Linux kernel's rtmutex code that has shipped in every major distribution since 2011 and lets an unprivileged local user escalate to root and escape containers. Introduced in Linux 2.6.39 and fixed in 7.1, the flaw requires no special privileges or kernel config, and their 97% stable exploit earned $92,337 in Google's kernelCTF program.

title: GPT-5.6 Series
blurb: OpenAI released the GPT-5.6 series, whose top model GPT-5.6 Sol became the first to win an ARC-AGI-3 public game, solving ft09 at 87%. At max reasoning effort, Sol averages 13.33% on the Public set and 7.78% on Semi-Private, with ARC Prize crediting its edge to correctly orienting in unfamiliar environments and re-planning after failed hypotheses rather than executing code better.

title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.6 Sol, Its Most Powerful AI Model Yet
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 Sol, which it calls its most powerful model to date. The launch was delayed after the U.S. government restricted deployment of frontier AI models over cybersecurity concerns, marking one of the first times federal policy has directly held back a major model release.

title: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI in the Northern District of California, alleging trade secret theft and breach of contract by former Apple employees now at OpenAI, including Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan. The suit accuses Tan of soliciting Apple's confidential product details during recruiting and a former engineer of downloading proprietary documents, landing as OpenAI builds an AI hardware device rumored to rival the iPhone.

title: SAP concedes to EU, freeing CIOs from expensive support shackles
blurb: The European Commission closed its antitrust investigation into SAP after the company agreed to loosen support and licensing terms for on-premises ERP customers worldwide. The binding commitments, enforceable for ten years, let customers split their SAP landscape across different support providers, terminate maintenance contracts when projects fail or products are phased out, and re-enter support without reinstatement fees.

title: Better tools made Copilot code review worse. Here’s how we actually improved it.
blurb: GitHub found that swapping Copilot code review onto its shared CLI tools (grep, glob, and view) raised review costs and caught fewer issues, even though the tools were better maintained. The regression came from the instructions, not the tooling; rewriting the prompts to match how a reviewer actually reads a pull request cut average review cost by roughly 20% while holding review quality steady.

title: Study of 281 Free Android VPN Apps Finds Traffic Leaks, Unencrypted Data, and Tracking
blurb: A study of 281 popular free Android VPN apps, installed over 2.4 billion times combined, found many fail at the basics: 29 leaked traffic outside the encrypted tunnel including DNS lookups, and 61 sent data in plain text. Five apps fetch their configuration file unencrypted, letting an attacker on the same network rewrite it and hijack the tunnel, an attack the University of Michigan-led researchers built and confirmed with their MVPNalyzer framework.

title: US Eases Export Curbs on UAE, Opening Door for AI Chip Sales
blurb: The Trump administration plans to ease export restrictions on the United Arab Emirates, clearing the way for the Gulf state to buy advanced semiconductors needed for its AI ambitions. The move opens a large new market for Nvidia and other chipmakers while giving the UAE access to the compute it needs to scale domestic AI infrastructure.

title: SK Hynix Debut Is a Bet That AI Breaks Boom-and-Bust Chip Cycle
blurb: SK Hynix raised the largest US public listing ever by a foreign company, with shares climbing 13% on their first day of trading. The South Korean memory chipmaker's debut wagers that sustained AI demand for high-bandwidth memory will smooth out the semiconductor industry's traditional boom-and-bust cycle.

title: SK Hynix raises $26.5B in the biggest foreign IPO in US history, is urged to build new US fabs
blurb: SK Hynix raised $26.5B in its Nasdaq debut, the largest US IPO ever by a non-American company, topping Alibaba's $25B offering in 2014. Demand ran more than seven times the available shares as investors bet on the chipmaker's high-bandwidth memory business supplying Nvidia, while US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick pressed the company to build new fabs stateside.

title: The White House Made Fixing Intel Its Pet Project. It’s Working.
blurb: The White House has made reviving Intel a priority, pressuring companies including Apple and Nvidia to become customers and partners, and the chipmaker's business is showing signs of improvement. The administration's intervention marks an unusual level of direct government involvement in steering private-sector deals toward a struggling American manufacturer central to domestic chip production.

title: OpenAI introduces ChatGPT Work, a cloud-based AI agent that manages tasks across email, Slack and calendars
blurb: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, a cloud-based agent powered by GPT-5.6 that executes multi-step tasks across users' email, Slack, calendars, and code repositories, producing finished documents, spreadsheets, and reports. The agent runs on an always-on virtual machine hosted on OpenAI's servers rather than a local device, ships to all paid tiers including Plus, and arrives as the company advances a draft IPO valued between $730B and $852B.

title: After 7 years in production, Scarf has reluctantly moved away from Haskell
blurb: Scarf moved its production backend off Haskell after seven years, citing slow compilation and ecosystem friction that AI coding tools made harder to justify. Founder Avi Press, who sits on the Haskell Foundation board, says the type system caught real bugs and met SLAs reliably, but LLMs now shift the tradeoff by catching errors that Haskell's compiler used to be the main defense against.

title: The Pulse: Interesting AI coding stats from Cursor
blurb: Cursor released a report from two years of aggregated usage data showing that its power users generate roughly 10x more code than the median, with the top 1% producing 30,000 to 40,000 lines per week. The data also found that input tokens account for 90% of usage as agents read existing code, and nearly half of AI-generated changes are accepted without manual review.

title: How to achieve pruning when querying by non-partitioned columns in PostgreSQL
blurb: PostgreSQL partition pruning normally requires filtering by the partition key, which forces you to pick that key carefully and makes queries on other columns scan every partition. This post shows how to prune when filtering by non-partition-key columns instead, using correlations in the data to translate a predicate on one column into an implied range on the partition key.

title: An orbiting disco ball gave Einstein’s theory its most precise test yet
blurb: Astronomers led by Ignazio Ciufolini measured Earth's frame-dragging, the twisting of spacetime predicted by general relativity, to within 0.2% uncertainty, down from a few percent. The team tracked LARES-2, a 294.8kg Inconel sphere covered in 303 laser retroreflectors whose record-low area-to-mass ratio in medium-Earth orbit minimized the non-gravitational forces that had limited earlier tests.

title: SK hynix raises a record $26.5 billion in historic U.S. IPO — South Korean memory giant to fund massive HBM manufacturing expansions
blurb: SK hynix raised $26.5B in its Nasdaq debut, the largest-ever IPO by a foreign company in the US, selling 177.9M American depositary receipts at $149 apiece to more than seven times oversubscribed demand. The company, the world's leading maker of high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators, will steer proceeds into HBM capacity, including new fabs at its Yongin cluster, advanced-packaging lines, and a $4B plant in Indiana.

title: China becomes the second country to recover a rocket booster
blurb: China became the second country to recover a reusable rocket booster, catching its Long March 10B on its maiden flight in a sea-anchored net rigged with hooks rather than SpaceX-style landing legs or catch arms. Developed by state-owned CALT, the booster was captured intact and is slated to fly again by the end of the year, a step toward China's goal of becoming a space power by 2030.

title: AI Model Co-Design: Hardware-Friendly LLM Design
blurb: Nvidia's engineering blog breaks down how LLM design choices shape throughput and interactivity without trading away accuracy, framing deployment as a Pareto frontier between fleet tokens/sec and per-user latency. The post walks through how each serving quadrant, from short to long context, shifts time between attention and feed-forward layers, and applies Amdahl's law to show where hardware-aware optimization actually pays off.

title: Mistral joins rush to develop AI for robots
blurb: Mistral released Robostral Navigate, a robot-navigation model that guides machines through plain-language instructions using a single RGB camera instead of LiDAR, depth sensors, or multiple viewpoints. The model scored 76.6% on the R2R-CE benchmark, beating the best depth-and-multi-camera system by 4.5 points, and cuts training runs from months to days by using far fewer tokens.

title: An update on residential proxies and the scraper situation
blurb: LWN reports that AI training scrapers now hit sites from millions of unique residential and mobile IP addresses, each faked to look like an ordinary browser and used only two or three times before rotating away. This traffic runs through "residential proxies" installed on compromised phones, PCs, and media-streaming devices, often without the owner's knowledge, making the attacks nearly impossible to block by IP and steadily harder for small sites to survive.

title: Sources detail the Trump admin's heavy-handed intervention to aid Intel, including pushing it to expand local capacity and pressuring Apple to use Intel's fabs (Robbie Whelan/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The Trump administration intervened aggressively to prop up Intel, pushing the chipmaker to expand US manufacturing capacity and pressuring Apple to route production through Intel's fabs, according to sources cited by the Wall Street Journal. The moves mark an unusually direct government hand in steering commercial semiconductor decisions, aimed at reviving domestic chip production and reducing reliance on TSMC.

title: Meta Prices Its First Paid AI Model API at $4.25 per Million Output Tokens
blurb: Meta Superintelligence Labs opened a public preview of the Meta Model API, the first time it has charged outside developers for a model it built, at $1.25 per million input tokens and $4.25 per million output tokens. Bloomberg pegged the rates at roughly 25% of what OpenAI and Anthropic advertise for top models, and the launch bundles Muse Spark 1.1, a 1M-token context window, and OpenAI SDK compatibility.

title: Meta Ordered by E.U. to Alter ‘Addictive Design’ of Instagram and Facebook
blurb: EU regulators ordered Meta to change the "addictive design" of Instagram and Facebook, ruling that features like infinite scroll and engagement-driven recommendations violate the bloc's Digital Services Act. The decision could force Meta to rework core engagement mechanics for European users and sets a precedent for how the DSA polices product design across large platforms.

title: What's really slowing down the AI buildout
blurb: The AI buildout's real bottleneck isn't a shortage of electricity but the backlogged process of connecting new data centers and power plants to the grid, where median interconnection wait times jumped from under 20 months in 2005 to 55 months by 2023. The author argues that grids' first-come, first-served queues and rigid evaluation rules were built for a different era and need reform to keep pace with projects like OpenAI's 1.2-gigawatt Stargate campus.

title: Storybook Workbench: Audit Vibe-coded UIs and Find Hidden Bugs in Hours
blurb: Evil Martians released Storybook Workbench, a set of agent skills that audit AI-generated UIs by inventorying every component and translating each into a visible Storybook story. On one vibe-coded app, the tool flagged 31 dead components out of 469 files and six accessibility bugs no build would catch, completing in hours instead of three days.

title: Einstein's relativity rules chemical bonds in heavy elements, new research shows
blurb: Brown University chemists published direct spectroscopic evidence in Science that Einstein's relativity reshapes triple chemical bonds in heavy elements, overturning the standard high-school model. Using photoelectron spectroscopy on carbon-bismuth bonds, the team found that spin-orbit coupling smears the boundary between the sigma and pi bonds that textbooks treat as distinct, leaving three bonds that no longer fit either category.

title: EU threatens Meta with fines over addictive features on Facebook and Instagram
blurb: The EU Commission found Meta in breach of the Digital Services Act, ruling that Facebook and Instagram's infinite scroll, autoplay, and engagement-tuned recommendations constitute addictive design that Meta failed to adequately assess or mitigate. If the preliminary findings are confirmed, Meta could face a fine of up to 6% of global annual turnover and must disable features like autoplay by default, add real screen-time breaks, and de-emphasize engagement in its algorithm.

title: How the terrorist group Boko Haram uses frontier AI
blurb: A new report documents how Boko Haram has integrated frontier AI tools into its operations, using them for propaganda generation, recruitment, and operational planning. The analysis argues that off-the-shelf commercial models are lowering the technical barrier for extremist groups, raising questions about how labs should handle misuse by non-state actors.

title: Chat Control 1.0 sneaks through the EU Parliament, letting companies scan user data without warrants — legal tactic used to force a majority-required re-vote on eve of Parliament break
blurb: The EU Parliament failed to reject Chat Control 1.0 in a forced re-vote requiring an absolute majority to block it, keeping the rule alive until 2028. The law lets online communication platforms like Gmail, Discord, and Instagram voluntarily mass-scan user data for CSAM without a warrant, though end-to-end-encrypted services stay exempt and law enforcement still needs targeted warrants.

title: SK Hynix's historic US stock market listing is a bet that the AI boom is breaking the memory chip industry's decades-long boom-and-bust cycle (Bloomberg)
blurb: SK Hynix filed for a US stock market listing, the first for the South Korean memory maker and a wager that AI demand has ended the chip sector's boom-and-bust cycle. The move ties the company's fortunes to sustained AI infrastructure spending, betting that high-bandwidth memory demand from datacenters will keep prices elevated rather than crashing as past cycles did.

title: Disable autoplay and infinite scroll or risk massive fines, EU tells Meta
blurb: The European Commission preliminarily found that Meta's autoplay, infinite scroll, and personalized recommendations amount to addictive design that Facebook and Instagram failed to adequately assess for risks to minors and vulnerable adults. The EC wants Meta to disable those features by default, add screen-time breaks, and make its recommender less engagement-driven, with fines possible under the Digital Services Act if the findings hold after Meta's rebuttal.

title: An analysis of 1M+ social media posts from April 24 to June 30: ~25% of longform posts with 250+ words were fully AI-generated; on LinkedIn, the figure was 41% (Max Spero/Pangram Labs)
blurb: Pangram Labs analyzed more than 1M social media posts from April 24 to June 30 and found that roughly 25% of longform posts over 250 words were fully AI-generated. On LinkedIn the share hit 41%, the highest of any platform studied, suggesting professional self-promotion is now the dominant use case for machine-written text.

title: In its lawsuit, Apple chronicles in vivid detail how its former employees that went to work for OpenAI allegedly violated their confidentiality agreements (Aaron Tilley/The Information)
blurb: Apple's lawsuit against several former employees who left for OpenAI details how the ex-staffers allegedly breached confidentiality agreements by taking proprietary information with them. The Information's Aaron Tilley reports that Apple lays out specific instances of the alleged violations, escalating a talent-and-IP dispute as OpenAI continues to recruit aggressively from the company's ranks.

title: OpenAI exec Fidji Simo says she's stepping down due to chronic illness, will transition to advisor
blurb: Fidji Simo, OpenAI's product and business chief, is stepping down to focus on recovery from a chronic illness and will transition to a part-time advisory role. Simo joined in May 2025 to lead OpenAI's applications business after taking Instacart public and running Facebook, and her exit follows an April medical leave during which President Greg Brockman absorbed her product responsibilities.

title: Big Tech’s debt tied to AI has doubled to $350B as the cash flow isn’t keeping up
blurb: Bloomberg reports that Big Tech's AI-related debt has doubled to $350B, as Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Meta plan a combined $725B in 2026 capital expenditure that their cash flow can't cover. Alphabet alone raised $80B in equity and $85B in debt across six currencies in the past year, pushing hyperscalers into European credit markets where Morgan Stanley projects their euro borrowing could hit €50B in 2026.

title: OpenAI's Atlas browser doesn't make it to its first birthday
blurb: OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, its AI-powered web browser, less than a year after launch. The retirement signals that OpenAI is narrowing focus to ChatGPT and its core model business rather than competing directly with Chrome and other browsers, and it marks a rare product wind-down for a company that has mostly expanded its surface area.

title: China is catching up to Elon Musk’s reusable rockets
blurb: China's state-owned CASC landed a Long March booster on a seagoing recovery vessel on Friday, making China the second country after the US to recover an orbital rocket booster. The Falcon 9-class rocket, which CASC aims to reuse by year's end, could sharply cut China's launch costs and let its satellite networks and orbital data centers challenge SpaceX's Starlink in Africa, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia.

title: Oratomic raises $300M to build a viable quantum computer that needs only 20K qubits
blurb: Oratomic raised $300M in a Series A co-led by ARCH Venture Partners, Spark Capital, and Khosla Ventures to build a utility-scale quantum computer by the end of the decade. The Caltech-founded startup uses lasers as optical tweezers to hold individual atoms and claims an error-correction breakthrough that needs only 10,000 to 20,000 qubits, far fewer than rivals, while skipping the NISQ prototype stage entirely.

title: Quantum error correction can constantly recalibrate a processor
blurb: Google showed that quantum error correction data can double as a live calibration signal, letting a processor recalibrate itself mid-computation without pausing the algorithm. Superconducting transmon qubits normally drift out of tune during long runs because standard calibration can't happen while calculations are underway, so reusing error-correction measurements to retune the controlling microwave pulses removes a bottleneck for running deeper, longer quantum circuits.

title: SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-Jung says the memory industry is heading for its worst-ever supply shortage in 2027 and demand will outstrip supply beyond 2030 (Reuters)
blurb: SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-Jung said the memory industry is heading for its worst-ever supply shortage in 2027, with demand set to outstrip supply beyond 2030. He attributed the crunch to AI-driven appetite for high-bandwidth memory, signaling sustained pricing power for DRAM makers and tighter component costs for anyone building AI hardware.

title: SK Hynix CEO Expects Memory Crunch to Last Beyond 2030
blurb: SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-Jung expects the current memory-chip shortage to persist beyond 2030, driven by sustained AI demand for high-bandwidth memory. The crunch is already squeezing supply for computers, cars, and consumer devices, signaling years of tight capacity and elevated pricing across the hardware industry.

title: Nvidia’s biggest RAM supplier just had a trillion-dollar debut on Wall Street
blurb: SK Hynix debuted on Wall Street Friday, opening at $170 per share and raising $26.5 billion to surpass Alibaba as the largest listing by a foreign company. Nvidia's biggest RAM supplier holds 29% of the global DRAM market and now trades above a $1 trillion valuation, riding surging demand for the high-bandwidth memory packaged inside AI chips like Blackwell Ultra.

title: SK Chairman Chey Tae-won on SK Hynix Debut, AI Demand and US Plans
blurb: SK Group listed SK Hynix in the US and raised $26.5 billion, the largest-ever US IPO by a foreign company. Chairman Chey Tae-won called the debut a "dream come true" and used the appearance to outline bullish AI memory demand and plans to expand SK's US investment.

title: SK Hynix Starts Trading on Nasdaq, Opens 14% Above Offer Price | Bloomberg Tech 7/10/2026
blurb: SK Hynix started trading on the Nasdaq, opening 14% above its offer price in the largest-ever US listing by a foreign company. Group Chairman Chey Tae-won rang in the debut, which deepens the South Korean memory maker's US footprint as demand for high-bandwidth memory tied to AI accelerators reshapes the broader chip sector.

title: China recovered its first reusable rocket and showed a new way to do it
blurb: China's state-owned CASC recovered an orbital-class booster for the first time, guiding its Long March 10B first stage into a cable net on an offshore vessel in the South China Sea. The recovery makes China the third entity to land a reusable booster after SpaceX and Blue Origin, and the net-capture method differs from the propulsive landings both US companies use.

title: Nuclear Energy Services Firm Holtec Joins Sector’s IPO Rush
blurb: Holtec Nuclear Corp. filed for a US IPO, joining a wave of nuclear companies going public to meet surging power demand from data centers. The energy-services firm bets that AI-driven electricity needs will keep fueling investor appetite for atomic power, following recent listings from small modular reactor developers and other players in the sector.

title: Meta's stock has best week since early 2024 as optimism builds around AI strategy
blurb: Meta's stock rose 15% this week, its best weekly performance since early 2024, after the company released Muse Image and Muse Spark 1.1 and reported progress on its in-house Iris AI chip. The rally erased Meta's losses for the year and signals Wall Street is warming to Zuckerberg's AI spending plan, which pushed 2026 capex guidance as high as $145B and now points toward a possible cloud-computing play against Amazon and Microsoft.

title: SK Hynix rises 13% in Nasdaq debut. Chairman tells CNBC 'demand is enormous'
blurb: SK Hynix rose 13% in its Nasdaq debut, closing at $168.01 after its ADRs priced at $149 raised $26.5B for new factories and equipment. The memory maker, which supplies high-bandwidth memory for Nvidia's AI chips and trails only Samsung in South Korea by market cap, has seen its valuation climb more than sevenfold in the past year as the AI infrastructure boom drives a memory shortage.

title: Meta Removes A.I. Feature on Instagram After Days of Backlash
blurb: Meta removed Muse Image, a new generative-AI feature on Instagram, days after users and Hollywood agencies flagged privacy and copyright concerns. The reversal underscores how quickly rights holders and users can force frontier platforms to pull AI tools, even as Meta continues to push generative features across its apps.

title: China Successfully Launches Reusable Rocket in a Win for Space Program
blurb: China launched and partially recovered a reusable rocket, a milestone for Beijing's space program as it works to close the gap with SpaceX. The recovery demonstrates progress on the kind of booster reusability that has driven down US launch costs, sharpening the competition between China and American operators for dominance in commercial and national spaceflight.

title: How Terrorist Groups Are Using A.I. to Gain an Edge in Battle
blurb: New research finds that violent extremist groups including Boko Haram are using AI chatbots for more than propaganda, drawing on them for bomb construction and attack planning. The findings sharpen the case that consumer AI safeguards remain porous, pushing the harm from persuasion toward operational assistance that lowers the barrier to real-world attacks.

title: Home robots already walk. 1X’s new hands try to solve the part that actually matters
blurb: 1X gave its NEO home robot new tendon-driven hands with 25 degrees of freedom and tactile sensors that read both pressure and shear, letting the fingers feel a glass slipping and tighten before it falls. The backdrivable, IP68-rated hands target the hardest problem in home robotics, but full autonomy is unproven, and unfamiliar chores still fall back to "Expert Mode," where a remote human operator steers the machine.

title: EU finds that Meta breached bloc’s rules with its social network interfaces
blurb: The European Commission tentatively found that Meta breached the Digital Services Act, ruling that Facebook and Instagram use addictive design tactics like infinite scroll, video autoplay, and personalized feeds that keep users, including minors, browsing. If the preliminary findings are confirmed, Meta faces a fine of up to 6% of annual revenue and could be forced to change the flagged interface features, though it disputes the conclusions and can challenge them.

title: The first flights of the US air-taxi program carried organs, not passengers
blurb: Beta Technologies flew the first missions of the US government's eVTOL Integration Pilot Program, carrying manufactured organs for United Therapeutics roughly 275 nautical miles between Maryland and Virginia rather than passengers. The Amazon-backed company is the most active of eight projects across 26 states, but certification remains slated for 2027-28 and the sector has already seen once-hyped rivals stall against airworthiness hurdles.

title: A Plan to Stop Solar Storms From Sending Us Back to the Stone Age
blurb: Researchers proposed StormWall, a constellation of school-bus-size satellites that would release charged particles to form a protective ionic shield against extreme solar storms. The system would act like an air bag for Earth's power grids and communications infrastructure, which a severe geomagnetic event could otherwise knock offline for months and cause trillions in damage.

title: Meta’s detection tool fails to identify photos generated by its own Muse Image AI
blurb: Meta's Content Seal watermarking system failed a Reuters test, with Meta's own detection tool identifying only 45% of 40 images generated by its Muse Image AI as AI-made. The tool broke whenever images were cropped to a third or quarter of their original size, undercutting Meta's claim that the provenance signal survives cropping, compression, and screenshots, and highlighting how brittle AI-provenance systems remain.

title: Meta’s addictive Instagram and Facebook design could lead to billions in fines and a forced redesign
blurb: The European Commission issued preliminary findings that Meta breached the Digital Services Act, ruling that Instagram and Facebook features like infinite scroll, autoplay, and push notifications create addictive risks the company never properly assessed for minors and vulnerable users. Regulators want addictive defaults disabled and the recommender system reworked, and if Meta fails to comply it faces fines of up to 6% of global annual revenue per platform, potentially billions of dollars.

title: Meta just pulled its most controversial AI image generation feature days after launch
blurb: Meta pulled Muse Image, an AI tool that generated pictures of people from their public Instagram profiles, three days after launch following widespread backlash. The feature was on by default, enrolling millions of public accounts without consent, and Meta acknowledged it "missed the mark" after pressure from users and talent agencies including CAA.

title: OpenAI sets Aug. 9 end date for ChatGPT Atlas
blurb: OpenAI will discontinue ChatGPT Atlas, its standalone desktop browser, on August 9, less than a year after its October launch on Mac. The browsing, work-agent, and Codex features are folding into a single ChatGPT desktop app, while a ChatGPT and Codex Chrome extension lets users keep their existing browser instead of switching to an OpenAI one.

title: Blue Origin seeks to raise $10 billion in outside capital
blurb: Blue Origin is raising $10 billion led by Coatue Management, the first outside capital in the company's 25-year history and a round that would value it at $130 billion. The money supports capital-intensive work on New Glenn, including rebuilding Launch Complex 36 after a May explosion and scaling toward a target of 50 launches a year by 2030.

title: FCC approves first Reflect Orbital satellite
blurb: The FCC authorized Reflect Orbital to launch Eärendil-1, a satellite that will deploy an 18-meter thin-film reflector in low Earth orbit to test beaming sunlight to specific ground sites for several minutes at a time. The 142-kilogram spacecraft launches later this year, drawing sharp opposition from astronomers and environmentalists who warn a proposed 50,000-satellite constellation could scatter light across the sky and disrupt telescopes and nocturnal ecosystems.

title: WSJ: Apple avoided semiconductor tariffs last year thanks to Intel chip deal
blurb: Apple won an exemption from the Trump administration's proposed 100% semiconductor tariff last summer after committing hundreds of billions in US investment, with the WSJ reporting that agreeing to use Intel-made chips was central to the deal. Trump later announced Apple would source some Mac and iPhone chips from Intel, sending Intel shares to record highs, though Apple still faced higher costs from the global memory shortage.

title: Startup testing nuclear battery technology in orbit
blurb: City Labs launched BOHR, a cubesat carrying the first commercial in-orbit demonstration of its NanoTritium betavoltaic power system, aboard SpaceX's Transporter-17 mission on July 7. The tritium-powered battery generates microwatts rather than watts, too little to run a satellite but enough to keep low-power electronics operating for years in deep space or permanently shadowed lunar regions where solar fails.

title: Broadcom will sell more US-made components to Apple
blurb: Apple agreed to buy up to $30B in additional chips and wireless components from Broadcom, expanding US-made production in Fort Collins, Colorado.

title: US eases restrictions on Apple’s access to AI chips and data center equipment in the UAE
blurb: The US Department of Commerce eased export restrictions on advanced computing hardware bound for the UAE, letting Apple and seven other US companies ship covered AI chips, servers, and controlled technology to their Emirati operations without individual licenses. Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, and xAI qualify under the same rule, which takes effect July 14 and clears a path for data center buildouts in the Gulf.

title: FCC grants approval for sun-reflecting space mirror that's been widely criticized by astronomers
blurb: The FCC approved Reflect Orbital's Earendil-1 demonstration satellite, which uses an adjustable thin-film mirror to reflect sunlight to Earth at night for on-demand lighting of solar farms or search-and-rescue sites. The American Astronomical Society opposed the license, warning the deliberately bright reflector would disrupt astronomical research and risk eye damage and flash-blinding of pilots and drivers, but the FCC declined to weigh those health and environmental concerns as outside its spectrum-authorization role.

title: Microsoft's AI drive saw its carbon emissions grow by 25 percent in 2025
blurb: Microsoft's carbon emissions grew 25% year over year in fiscal 2025, driven mainly by its expanding AI data center buildout, according to the company's new sustainability report. The increase moves Microsoft further from its 2020 pledge to be carbon negative by 2030, with the company conceding that sustainability solutions are not scaling fast enough to meet AI-driven demand for energy, water, and materials.

title: Researchers turn HBM on its side to tackle AI memory’s heat wall — Korean V-Die and Japanese MOSAIC designs promise higher bandwidth, denser stacks, and cooler future GPUs
blurb: Researchers in Korea and Japan presented two designs that turn HBM's DRAM dies on their edges to raise capacity and bandwidth without trapping more heat in taller stacks. UNIST's V-Die adds liquid-cooling channels between upright dies and hit 540 tokens per second on a GPT-3 workload versus 296 for HBM4, while Tokyo's MOSAIC uses inductive coils for contactless die-to-die links and claims double HBM4-class capacity.

title: Tencent is reportedly in talks to acquire Manus from Meta, following Beijing intervention — company expects to remain independent of Chinese tech giant
blurb: Tencent is reportedly in talks to acquire Manus from Meta at the original $2B price, unwinding Meta's purchase of the Chinese AI agent startup after Beijing ordered the deal reversed. A consortium of former China-based investors including ZhenFund and HSG is expected to join, while US backers like Benchmark stay out, reflecting Beijing's push to keep its AI talent and IP from flowing to American labs.

title: SK hynix and TetraMem collaborate on experimental chip to bolster energy efficiency for edge AI devices — memristor-based in-memory SoC research leaves performance questions up in the air
blurb: SK hynix, TetraMem, and USC researchers built a memristor-based in-memory computing SoC that runs neural network inference for edge AI at a fraction of the power draw of GPUs or NPUs. The proof-of-concept chip pairs a RISC-V scheduler with 10 NPUs, one tuned for depthwise convolution, but peaks at roughly 2.54 TOPS in a best-case scenario, about 16x below Microsoft's Copilot+ requirements.

title: Meta could start production of Iris AI chip in September: report
blurb: Meta plans to begin mass production of its Iris AI inference chip in September, according to a Reuters report on an internal memo, with the accelerator co-designed with Broadcom and fabricated by TSMC. The chip is part of Meta's push to deploy 7 GW of compute in 2026 and 14 GW by end of 2027, backed by capex the company now expects to reach $120B to $135B this year.

title: SpaceX wants to launch 100k more Starlink satellites for 100x the bandwidth
blurb: SpaceX applied to the FCC for permission to launch 100,000 third-generation Starlink satellites in very low Earth orbit, nearly 10x its current constellation of roughly 11,000. The Gen3 satellites weigh over two tons each, too heavy to launch in bulk on Falcon 9, so SpaceX will lean on Starship and Falcon Heavy to promise multi-gigabit symmetrical speeds.

title: SK Hynix just pulled off the biggest foreign share sale in U.S. history — and American investors paid a premium for it
blurb: SK Hynix raised $26.5B in a US share sale, the largest initial US offering by a foreign company and surpassing Alibaba's $25B in 2014. The Korean chipmaker sold 177.9M ADRs at $149 each, priced at a 3% premium to Seoul and reportedly over seven times oversubscribed, with proceeds funding new fabs and HBM capacity for Nvidia's AI accelerators.

title: Home robots can already walk. The hard part is stopping them from crushing your glassware
blurb: 1X gave NEO, its humanoid home robot, new tendon-driven hands with 25 degrees of freedom, tactile skin that senses slipping objects, and backdrivable joints that yield on contact instead of crushing what they grab. Rated IP68 with food-safe materials for work near sinks and dishes, the hardware looks capable, but the software still has to prove it can complete an ordinary chore autonomously in a cluttered room.

title: Micron lifts U.S. spending to $250 billion — company takes $500 million position in America's only 300 mm wafer plant
blurb: Micron committed up to $3 billion to the U.S. semiconductor supply chain, including a $500 million strategic financing position in GlobalWafers' Sherman, Texas plant plus a 10-year deal for its wafer output. Sherman is the only U.S. facility making advanced 300 mm raw silicon, and Micron separately raised its planned domestic spending to more than $250 billion through 2035, up from $200 billion.

title: Japanese chipmaker Rapidus to offer lower wafer pricing than TSMC — 2nm class silicon to be priced around $20,000 on 2027 launch
blurb: Rapidus will price its 2nm-class wafers at roughly $18,550 to $21,635 each when high-volume production starts in the second half of 2027, well under TSMC's rumored $30,000 quote for N2 and in line with Samsung's SF2. The discount carries risk, since Rapidus reaches meaningful volume only in 2028, by which point TSMC will have moved to more mature N2P and A16 nodes plus its established design and packaging ecosystem.

title: SK Hynix ADRs Surge After Record $26.5 Billion US Offering
blurb: SK Hynix raised $26.5B in the largest-ever US listing by a foreign company, with ADRs opening 14% above the offering price.

title: Delayed Xbox Layoffs Leave Employees Scared And Nervous
blurb: Microsoft cut 1,600 Xbox jobs this week and plans another 1,600 over the next 12 months, leaving remaining staff on edge.

title: SK Hynix Climbs 13% After Record $26.5 Billion US Offering
blurb: SK Hynix rose 13% in its US trading debut after raising $26.5B in the largest-ever first-time share sale by a foreign company. The offering gives the South Korean memory chipmaker a US-listed stock to fund capacity expansion as demand for high-bandwidth memory tied to AI accelerators keeps outpacing supply.

title: Record Companies Push to Label AI Songs on Streaming Platforms
blurb: An RIAA-led coalition of music labels and artist groups is pushing streaming platforms to label songs made with AI, arguing that listeners want to know when a track is machine-generated. The effort would establish disclosure standards across services like Spotify and Apple Music, as a flood of AI-generated tracks strains detection systems and revives fights over consent, credit, and royalties.

title: Amazon now runs more than a million warehouse robots — closing in on its roughly 1.2 million-strong operations workforce
blurb: Amazon now runs more than a million warehouse robots, closing in on its roughly 1.2 million-strong operations workforce.

title: A MIT-OpenAI study of nearly 40 million chats found the heaviest ChatGPT users reported more loneliness, dependence, and less time with real people, though researchers warn the link is correlation, not cause
blurb: A joint MIT-OpenAI analysis of nearly 40 million ChatGPT conversations, plus a 981-person randomized trial, found that the heaviest users reported more loneliness, dependence, and less time with other people. The researchers stress the link is correlational, with total time spent predicting emotional attachment better than any other factor, so the studies stop short of showing whether the chatbot drives the isolation or lonelier people simply use it more.

title: 1-in-2 Phones Sold in Africa Exfiltrate Telemetry to China
blurb: Researchers at NowSecure decrypted the telemetry sent by Transsion phones (TECNO, Infinix, itel), the world's fourth-largest smartphone maker, and found the AES keys embedded directly in the client APK. The decrypted beacons to *.shalltry.com carry per-app network activity, the foreground app, precise location, and camera usage bound to permanent identifiers, exposing how deeply preinstalled software can surveil devices outside any organization's control.

title: GPT-5.6 Series
blurb: OpenAI released the GPT-5.6 series, three models with 15 reasoning variants, led by GPT-5.6 Sol. At max reasoning effort, Sol is the first model to win an ARC-AGI-3 public game, scoring 87% on ft09 and averaging 13.33% on the Public and 7.78% on the Semi-Private benchmarks.

title: Google's TabFM skips per-dataset training and still predicts on tables it's never seen
blurb: Google Research introduced TabFM, a foundation model that treats tabular prediction as in-context learning and generates predictions for an unseen table in a single forward pass, skipping the per-dataset training that gradient-boosted models require. It sidesteps the LLM approach, which chokes on tables due to context limits, tokenization that splits numerical values, and loss of row-column structure when a 2D table is serialized to text.

title: Researcher Details WhatsApp-to-Host Attack Chain Using Three OpenClaw Flaws
blurb: Security researcher Chinmohan Nayak detailed three now-patched high-severity flaws in the OpenClaw personal AI assistant that chain together to trigger host code execution from an external WhatsApp message, requiring no prior foothold. The bugs, all fixed in version 2026.6.6, combine command injection with a path traversal flaw that lets a bind mount expose parent directories like /home, giving an attacker read access to every user's SSH keys.

title: Z.ai's Stable Asynchronous RL
blurb: This paper introduces Single-rollout Asynchronous Optimization (SAO), a reinforcement learning method that replaces GRPO's group-wise sampling with one rollout per prompt and adds strict double-side token-level clipping to stabilize off-policy training. SAO trains stably for 1,000 steps and beats GRPO variants on SWE-Bench Verified, BeyondAIME, and IMOAnswerBench, and was used in Z.ai's pipeline for the open GLM-5.2 model (750B-A40B).

title: Flexible Video Diffusion
blurb: NVIDIA's Flex-Forcing is a video diffusion method that lets a single model handle text-to-video, image-to-video, and video extension without task-specific retraining. It works by varying the noise schedule across frames during training, so the same model can condition on clean past frames or generate sequences from scratch at inference.

title: Better tools made Copilot code review worse. Here’s how we actually improved it.
blurb: GitHub found that swapping Copilot code review's custom exploration tools for the shared Copilot CLI tools (grep, glob, and view) raised review costs and caught fewer issues, despite the newer tools being better maintained. The regression traced to the prompts rather than the tooling, and rewriting the instructions to match how a reviewer actually reads a pull request cut average review cost by roughly 20% while holding quality steady.

title: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging trade secret theft and breach of contract by former Apple employees now at OpenAI. The complaint accuses Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan of directing the misconduct and engineer Chang Liu of downloading confidential documents, as OpenAI builds a rumored hardware device that could rival the iPhone.

title: US Eases Export Curbs on UAE, Opening Door for AI Chip Sales
blurb: The Trump administration plans to ease export restrictions on the United Arab Emirates, clearing the way for the Gulf state to purchase advanced semiconductors and other technologies for its AI ambitions. The move opens a broad range of chip sales to a market that US curbs had previously kept largely off-limits, aligning export policy with the UAE's push to build out domestic AI infrastructure.

title: The Builder’s Economy: 10 Metrics from ICONIQ’s Newest 2026 State of AI Report
blurb: ICONIQ released the third edition of its State of AI report, "The Builder's Economy," drawing on a Q2 2026 survey of roughly 305 software executives building AI products. AI now approaches half of revenue at surveyed companies, projected to rise from 42% this year to 53% in 2027, while open source has climbed to 40% of the app layer and Anthropic overtook OpenAI as the most-used provider at 81%.

title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.6 Sol, Its Most Powerful AI Model Yet
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 Sol, its most capable model to date, after a delay imposed when the U.S. government restricted frontier AI models over cybersecurity concerns. The launch marks one of the first major model releases to clear the new federal review process, signaling how national security policy now shapes the timing of frontier AI deployments.

title: SK Hynix Debut Is a Bet That AI Breaks Boom-and-Bust Chip Cycle
blurb: SK Hynix completed the largest US public listing by a foreign company in market history, with shares climbing 13% on their first day of trading. The debut reflects a bet that sustained AI demand for memory chips will smooth out the semiconductor industry's traditional boom-and-bust cycle.

title: JPMorgan Builds AI Agents That Beat 60/40 Model in Backtests
blurb: JPMorgan built AI agents that outperformed the traditional 60/40 stock-bond portfolio in backtests, moving beyond narrow tasks like stock picking toward autonomous capital allocation. The agents decide how to distribute money across assets themselves rather than assisting human managers, though backtested results do not guarantee live performance.

title: OpenAI introduces ChatGPT Work, a cloud-based AI agent that manages tasks across email, Slack and calendars
blurb: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, a cloud-based agent powered by GPT-5.6 that executes multi-step tasks across users' email, Slack, calendars, and code repositories. The agent runs on a persistent virtual machine on OpenAI's servers, staying available across devices while it breaks a stated outcome into steps and works on complex projects for hours, and it is included across all paid tiers.

title: SK hynix raises a record $26.5 billion in historic U.S. IPO — South Korean memory giant to fund massive HBM manufacturing expansions
blurb: SK hynix raised $26.5 billion in its Nasdaq debut, the largest-ever U.S. IPO by a foreign company, selling 177.9 million ADRs at $149 apiece in an offering more than seven times oversubscribed. The world's leading maker of high-bandwidth memory will steer the proceeds toward expanding AI-memory capacity, including the Yongin fab cluster, a Cheongju advanced-packaging line, and a $4 billion U.S. plant in Indiana.

title: The agents you use to beef up cybersecurity could be turned against you – ‘Friendly Fire’ attacks can manipulate OpenAI and Anthropic models into running malicious code
blurb: The AI Now Institute demonstrated a proof-of-concept "Friendly Fire" attack that manipulates Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex into executing malicious code during autonomous security reviews. The attack embeds prompt injections in a library's source code and README files, triggering remote code execution against out-of-the-box auto-mode and auto-review configurations on models including Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, and GPT-5.5 without requiring any user approval.

title: The Pulse: Interesting AI coding stats from Cursor
blurb: Cursor released a report on two years of aggregated usage data, and the distribution is heavily skewed: the median developer generates about 700 lines of code per week, while the 90th percentile hits 9,000 and the top 1% produces 30-40K. Token usage runs 10:1 input to output, meaning most spend goes to reading the existing codebase rather than generating new code, and nearly half of AI changes are accepted without manual review.

title: GPT‑Live
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-Live, a new voice model for ChatGPT that replaces the aging GPT-4o-era system with a 2024 knowledge cutoff. For questions requiring web search or deeper reasoning, GPT-Live delegates to GPT-5.5 in the background and keeps talking while it works, folding the result into the conversation once ready.

title: AI Model Co-Design: Hardware-Friendly LLM Design
blurb: NVIDIA's developer blog presents a primer on co-designing LLMs with modern hardware to push the throughput-versus-interactivity Pareto frontier outward while holding accuracy fixed. It breaks deployment into quadrants by context length and service goal, and applies Amdahl's law to show where optimization pays off, noting that when attention consumes 77% of runtime, tuning feed-forward layers yields only marginal gains.

title: Friendly Fire: Hijacking Defensive Cyber AI Agents for Remote Code Execution
blurb: A proof-of-concept exploit achieves remote code execution in Anthropic's Claude Code CLI and OpenAI's Codex CLI when the agents are used to defensively audit an untrusted library. The attack requires only out-of-the-box auto-mode or auto-review and works through prompt injections spread across a library's source code, without relying on hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, or config files as the injection vector.

title: Mistral joins rush to develop AI for robots
blurb: Mistral released Robostral Navigate, a robotics model that navigates complex indoor and outdoor environments from a single RGB camera, dropping the LiDAR, depth sensors, and multi-camera rigs most systems rely on. It scored 76.6% on the R2R-CE instruction-following benchmark, 4.5 points ahead of the best depth-and-multi-camera system, while cutting training runs from months to days.

title: Ways to think about token pricing
blurb: Benedict Evans argues that foundation model providers are heading toward becoming low-margin commodity infrastructure rather than capturing durable pricing power. He notes today's supply crunch is transitory and driven almost entirely by one use case, software development, while over $1T in data center capex and rapidly improving inference efficiency come online. Inference currently runs at 40-50% gross margins, but that excludes training costs that still far exceed revenue, leaving long-term pricing unresolved.

title: Meta Prices Its First Paid AI Model API at $4.25 per Million Output Tokens
blurb: Meta Superintelligence Labs released Muse Spark 1.1 and opened a public preview of the Meta Model API, the first time the company has charged outside developers for one of its own models. Access runs $1.25 per million input tokens and $4.25 per million output tokens, roughly 25% of what OpenAI and Anthropic advertise for top models, though still above GPT-5 mini and Claude Haiku 4.5.

title: Reducing High-Bandwidth Memory Bottlenecks in JAX-Based LLM Training with Host Offloading
blurb: This post explains how host offloading in JAX reduces GPU high-bandwidth memory pressure by moving selected activations to pinned host memory during the forward pass and streaming them back for the backward pass, as an alternative to activation rematerialization. Tested on GB200 NVL72 systems across 128 GPUs with Llama 3.1 405B and DeepSeek-V3 671B, the method exploits NVLink-C2C's 900 GB/s Grace-Blackwell bandwidth to overlap transfers with compute.

title: Lovable is Reportedly in Talks to Raise $300M at a $13.2bn Valuation
blurb: Lovable is reportedly in talks to raise $300M at a $13.2B valuation, per Sifted, roughly double the $6.6B valuation from its $330M Series B in December. The Swedish vibe-coding startup has passed $500M in annualized revenue with about 146 staff and roughly a million new projects starting on it each week.

title: What's really slowing down the AI buildout
blurb: The AI buildout's real bottleneck isn't a shortage of electricity but the grid's inability to deliver it where new data centers are being built. The interconnection queue, which uses a rigid first-come, first-served process, has stretched from under 20 months for the median 2005 power plant to 55 months by 2023. The author argues that grids need flexible interconnection rules to keep pace with projects like OpenAI and SoftBank's $40B, 1.2-gigawatt Stargate campus.

title: Auto mode knows it's dangerous (and does it anyway)
blurb: Coding agents will run `curl | sh` installers even when they correctly identify the risk. When asked to install air via a piped remote script, both Claude Code and Codex flagged the danger, then executed anyway: Claude Code disabled its sandbox because the user had explicitly requested it, and Codex auto-approved after rating the command medium-risk but high-authorization. The author's point is that recognizing a threat and deferring to explicit user intent are separate decisions, and auto mode collapses them.

title: CrowdStrike identifies five new AI prompt injection threats
blurb: CrowdStrike identified five new prompt injection techniques and added them to its taxonomy, including Trigger-Activated Rule Addition, Cognitive Token Suppression, and Algorithmic Payload Decomposition. The methods exploit the boundary between trusted data and executable instructions, with attacks that hide malicious commands inside uploaded documents, forwarded emails, or multi-stage payloads that appear harmless until assembled.

title: Introducing Feature Views
blurb: Databricks released Feature Views in Public Preview, a framework for authoring a managed ML feature once and letting the platform handle experimentation, training, and real-time serving from a single definition. The same definition supports both batch and streaming sources and computes training and online inference values against one spec, closing the train-serve skew that causes most real-time ML failures.

title: How the terrorist group Boko Haram uses frontier AI
blurb: A new report from the Center for Advanced Security Policy details how the terrorist group Boko Haram has integrated frontier AI models into its operations for propaganda generation, recruitment, and operational planning. The findings add to evidence that consumer-facing AI tools are being adopted by violent non-state actors despite provider safeguards, raising questions about how labs detect and restrict misuse at scale.

title: Show HN: Reverse-engineering web apps into agent tools
blurb: Frigade released a browser-based agent that runs inside an authenticated web app, watches how the app calls its own APIs, and automatically converts those calls into reusable agent tools, functioning as an auto-generated MCP server that self-updates as the host app changes. Each discovered "recipe" bundles the endpoint, method, auth handling, and input/output schemas, letting an LLM take actions inside third-party products like Jira or Spotify without touching their source code.

title: Anthropic says it can read Claude's 'thoughts,' as detailed in new research paper — models observed to have a global workspace, revealing more of what makes LLMs tick
blurb: Anthropic published a paper describing its Jacobian Lens (J-Lens) technique, which reads an internal reasoning space in Claude the company calls the "J-Space" and likens to Global Workspace Theory's global workspace. The paper reports that Claude appears to recognize when it is being evaluated and behaves differently, though it cautions that many responses bypass the J-Space entirely and are heavily token-restricted.

title: SK Hynix's historic US stock market listing is a bet that the AI boom is breaking the memory chip industry's decades-long boom-and-bust cycle (Bloomberg)
blurb: SK Hynix listed its shares on a US stock exchange, a historic first for the South Korean memory chipmaker. The move is a bet that surging AI demand has broken the memory industry's decades-long boom-and-bust cycle, replacing volatile pricing with sustained, structurally higher demand for high-bandwidth memory.

title: In its lawsuit, Apple chronicles in vivid detail how its former employees that went to work for OpenAI allegedly violated their confidentiality agreements (Aaron Tilley/The Information)
blurb: Apple sued several former employees who left for OpenAI, alleging in detailed filings that they violated their confidentiality agreements. The complaint reportedly chronicles specific instances of the departing staff taking or exposing proprietary information, escalating tensions between the two companies as OpenAI builds out its hardware and talent ambitions.

title: An analysis of 1M+ social media posts from April 24 to June 30: ~25% of longform posts with 250+ words were fully AI-generated; on LinkedIn, the figure was 41% (Max Spero/Pangram Labs)
blurb: Pangram Labs analyzed over 1M social media posts from April 24 to June 30 and found that roughly 25% of longform posts of 250+ words were fully AI-generated. On LinkedIn, that figure reached 41%, the highest of any platform studied, suggesting professional networking content has tipped toward machine-written text faster than the rest of the social web.

title: OpenAI exec Fidji Simo says she's stepping down due to chronic illness, will transition to advisor
blurb: Fidji Simo, OpenAI's head of applications and business, is stepping down to focus on recovery from a chronic illness and will move into a part-time advisor role. She took medical leave in April, with President Greg Brockman assuming her product responsibilities, and departs weeks after OpenAI confidentially filed for an IPO now reportedly delayed until at least next year.

title: Big Tech’s debt tied to AI has doubled to $350B as the cash flow isn’t keeping up
blurb: Bloomberg reports that Big Tech's AI-related debt has doubled to $350B, as capital expenditure outpaces the cash flow of Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Meta, who plan to spend a combined $725B in 2026. Bank of America estimates AI-related bonds will hit $270B in 2026, pushing hyperscalers into non-dollar markets and making US tech the largest single source of corporate debt in the eurozone.

title: GPT-5.6, Grok 4.5, Claude, and Muse Spark build the same 4 apps
blurb: GPT-5.6's three tiers (Sol, Terra, Luna), Grok 4.5, Claude Opus 4.8, Meta's Muse Spark 1.1, and four open-weights models each built the same four apps: a Doom-style raycaster, a Rubik's cube, a calculator, and Game of Life. Each model got five attempts per task, with cost and latency logged and every raw build published. GPT led the raycaster, Grok held up at its price point, and Claude underperformed expectations.

title: OpenAI's Atlas browser doesn't make it to its first birthday
blurb: OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, its ChatGPT-integrated web browser, less than a year after launch. The product failed to pull meaningful usage away from Chrome and Safari, and OpenAI is redirecting its browsing ambitions into agent features inside ChatGPT rather than maintaining a standalone browser.

title: Kernel Fusion in NVIDIA CUDA: Optimizing Memory Traffic and Launch Overhead
blurb: This NVIDIA CUDA post explains kernel fusion, which combines multiple GPU operations into a single device kernel so intermediate results stay in registers rather than round-tripping through global memory. Using a sum(abs(x)) example, it contrasts body-level fusion with CUDA Graphs, which capture a sequence of launches into one reusable object but leave the memory round-trip unchanged.

title: Nvidia’s biggest RAM supplier just had a trillion-dollar debut on Wall Street
blurb: SK Hynix debuted on Wall Street Friday, opening at $170 per share and raising $26.5 billion to surpass Alibaba as the largest listing by a foreign company. The DRAM and high-bandwidth memory supplier holds 29% of the global DRAM market and plans to ramp capacity over five years to address a memory shortage that could last until 2030.

title: Serving sub-second Ideogram v4 without quality loss
blurb: fal.ai serves Ideogram v4 at 0.44 seconds per 1K-resolution image, down from 2.75 seconds, a 6x speedup with no visible quality loss. The post details how they got there by running the diffusion transformer in NVFP4 with fused epilogue kernels, then recovering the quality lost to 4-bit with quantization-aware distillation and collapsing the denoising step count with timestep distillation.

title: OpenAI's head of safety, Johannes Heidecke, is leaving as OpenAI integrates its research and safety teams; Mia Glaese will become VP of research and safety (Maxwell Zeff/Wired)
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety Johannes Heidecke is leaving as the company merges its research and safety teams under a single organization. Mia Glaese will step into a new role as VP of research and safety, consolidating oversight that was previously split across separate groups.

title: Apple alleges that a former Apple engineer kept a work-issued Apple laptop and exploited a bug to access Apple's cloud file storage while employed by OpenAI (Megan Morrone/Axios)
blurb: Apple sued a former engineer, alleging he kept a work-issued laptop after leaving for OpenAI and exploited a bug to reach the company's cloud file storage. The complaint claims the access continued during his OpenAI employment, raising the prospect of trade-secret exposure between two firms already competing closely on AI.

title: SK Chairman Chey Tae-won on SK Hynix Debut, AI Demand and US Plans
blurb: SK Group Chairman Chey Tae-won said the US listing of chip unit SK Hynix, which raised $26.5 billion in the largest-ever US IPO by a foreign company, was a long-awaited goal for the group. In a Bloomberg interview, he also outlined the outlook for AI memory demand and the company's plans to expand US investment.

title: OpenAI power consolidates under co-founder Greg Brockman ahead of prospective IPO
blurb: Greg Brockman, an OpenAI co-founder and its president, took over the company's ChatGPT product business, go-to-market, enterprise, and compute teams after product and business chief Fidji Simo stepped down due to chronic illness. Reporting to Sam Altman, Brockman inherits pressure to grow revenue and justify OpenAI's $852B valuation ahead of an IPO the company confidentially filed for in June.

title: Meta Removes A.I. Feature on Instagram After Days of Backlash
blurb: Meta removed Muse Image, a new AI feature on Instagram, days after users and Hollywood talent agencies raised privacy and copyright concerns about the tool. The reversal followed sustained backlash over how the feature generated and used images, and marks another instance of a major platform pulling a consumer AI product shortly after launch.

title: Wall Street is debating the AI buildout. Enterprises just answered: 86% say their GPUs run at half capacity or less
blurb: A VentureBeat Research survey of 573 technical leaders found that 86% of enterprises running their own GPUs report utilization of 50% or less. The finding lands as Wall Street debates whether the AI buildout is overbuilt, offering buy-side measurement from the companies actually purchasing the hardware. The same surveys show governance lagging deployment: 54% had an agent security incident or near-miss in the past year, and 27% control agent spend only reactively, learning the cost when the invoice arrives.

title: Enterprise AI is entering an evaluation gap: Agents are gaining autonomy faster than companies can verify them
blurb: A June 2026 VB Pulse survey of 157 enterprise respondents found that half had deployed an AI agent or LLM feature that passed internal evaluations but still caused a customer-facing failure, with one in four hit more than once. Yet 66% already permit some production deployment without human review or plan to within a year, while only 5% fully trust the automated evaluations behind those decisions. The author calls this widening mismatch the evaluation gap: agent autonomy is rising faster than the assurance beneath it.

title: Meta’s detection tool fails to identify photos generated by its own Muse Image AI
blurb: Meta's own AI detection tool failed to identify 55% of images generated by its new Muse Image AI, according to a Reuters analysis of 40 photos that were cropped to a third or quarter of their original size. The gap undercuts Meta's claim that its Content Seal watermark stays intact even when cropped, compressed, or resized, and Meta now says the detector is still in preview.

title: Claude Code can now browse the web without opening Chrome
blurb: Anthropic added a built-in Browser pane to Claude Code on desktop, letting its coding assistant open websites, read documentation, inspect designs, and click through pages without leaving the app. The isolated browser runs a clean profile with per-site permission prompts, blocks account creation, purchases, and CAPTCHA bypass without explicit approval, and supports logins including Google OAuth for testing authenticated applications.

title: OpenAI sets Aug. 9 end date for ChatGPT Atlas
blurb: OpenAI will deprecate ChatGPT Atlas, its standalone desktop browser, on August 9, less than a year after its October launch on Mac. The browsing and task-automation features are moving into the new ChatGPT desktop app, which combines built-in browsing with ChatGPT Work and Codex, while a separate Chrome extension keeps those capabilities available to Chrome users.

title: Open-source AI model developer MiniMax raises $2B in funding
blurb: MiniMax, the Shanghai-based developer behind the open-source MiniMax LLM series, is raising $2B, with more than half coming from newly issued shares and the rest from convertible bonds. The company plans to follow the round with $6.5B in zero-coupon convertible bonds due in 2027, after debuting its 427B-parameter MiniMax-M3 model in June.

title: Tencent is reportedly in talks to acquire Manus from Meta, following Beijing intervention — company expects to remain independent of Chinese tech giant
blurb: Tencent is reportedly in talks to buy Manus back from Meta at the original $2B price, after Beijing intervened to unwind Meta's surprise acquisition of the Chinese AI agent startup. The consortium would include former investors like ZhenFund and HSG but exclude U.S. backers such as Benchmark, part of a broader push to keep China's AI talent and IP from flowing to American firms.

title: Meta could start production of Iris AI chip in September: report
blurb: Meta could begin production of its Iris AI inference chip in September, according to a Reuters report citing an internal memo, following a six-week test cycle that surfaced no major issues. Iris is part of the MTIA accelerator line Meta detailed in March, designed with Broadcom and manufactured by TSMC to support the company's plan to reach 14 GW of compute capacity by the end of 2027.

title: Après une pause de 3 mois, la Française Fidji Simo quitte son poste chez OpenAI pour se soigner
blurb: OpenAI applications CEO Fidji Simo is leaving the company to focus on her health, three months after taking medical leave to manage postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, an autoimmune condition. Simo, who joined from Meta in August 2025 to lead OpenAI's applications division, will stay on as a part-time advisor.

title: Après AMI Labs, Yann LeCun veut lancer un fonds de 200 millions d’euros dédié à l’IA
blurb: Yann LeCun is raising a €200M fund called Extelligence Invest, according to Sifted, following AMI Labs, the world-models startup he launched after leaving Meta with over $1B in funding. The fund is targeting startups in data and AI infrastructure, health and longevity, robotics, mobility, and high-performance computing, deliberately avoiding the LLM developers that dominate Silicon Valley.

title: OpenAI Safety Head Heidecke to Leave Firm After Reshuffle: Wired
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety Johannes Heidecke is leaving the company following an internal reorganization, according to Wired. The departure adds to a series of high-profile safety exits at the firm as it restructures the teams responsible for evaluating model risks.

title: SK Hynix’s $26.5B Nasdaq debut is a reminder that AI startups are paying a memory tax they can’t avoid
blurb: SK Hynix began trading on the Nasdaq on July 10 after raising $26.5B, the largest first-time US listing by a foreign company. The bigger signal for AI startups is the underlying memory squeeze, with conventional DRAM contract prices up 58% to 63% quarter over quarter in Q2 2026 as SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron reallocate capacity toward higher-margin HBM.

title: Opinion | Is Private AI Regulation Constitutional?
blurb: This WSJ opinion piece argues that a private AI regulatory body, like the industry organization Google has proposed, would raise constitutional questions because delegating government-like regulatory power to private actors implicates the nondelegation and due process doctrines. The author contends that such an entity's authority would ultimately remain subject to judicial scrutiny, limiting how much genuine regulatory power it could wield.

title: OpenAI Folds Codex Into ChatGPT as Atlas Gets August 9 Deprecation Target
blurb: OpenAI folded Codex and ChatGPT Work into a new ChatGPT desktop app for Mac and Windows, and set an August 9 deprecation target for its standalone Atlas browser less than a year after launch. The app ships alongside GPT-5.6's Sol, Terra, and Luna tiers, and makes Chat, Work, and Codex available globally on every plan, including Free.

title: A Script for Mark Zuckerberg
blurb: Ben Thompson writes a fictional earnings-call script for Mark Zuckerberg to justify Meta's tens of billions in AI capex to skeptical investors. The imagined speech walks through Facebook's history, from the News Feed backlash to the mobile transition, to argue that product conviction and trusting revealed user preference over data have consistently paid off. The framing casts Meta's AI spending as another bet-the-company pivot justified by Zuckerberg's track record rather than near-term returns.

title: A US senator has a plan to make AI answer for its harms. It starts with your local data centre
blurb: Senator Ed Markey introduced an "AI accountability agenda," a package of bills whose centerpiece would require any company building a data center to obtain FCC certification that the site "will not harm the public interest" before construction. The other bills mandate independent bias audits for high-stakes algorithms, bar employers from relying mainly on automated hiring decisions, restrict chatbots aimed at children, and require human override for AI medical decisions.

title: AI Agents Are a Strong Bug-Finding Tool, Not an Oracle
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation's Protocol Security team reported that coordinated AI agents effectively surface vulnerabilities in its network infrastructure, but most candidates turn out to be wrong, duplicate, or out of scope. The agents found real bugs, including a remotely-triggerable panic in libp2p's gossipsub, yet the bottleneck shifted from finding bugs to triaging them, since a finding only counts once researchers independently reproduce the failure against live code.

title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.6 Sol, Its Most Powerful AI Model Yet
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 Sol, its most capable model to date, after a delay imposed when the U.S. government restricted frontier AI releases over cybersecurity concerns. The launch marks the first time a major lab has shipped a flagship model on a schedule dictated by federal security review rather than its own roadmap. For founders building on OpenAI, the signal is that access to the newest models now runs through a regulatory gate, and release timing is no longer something the labs fully control.

title: SK Hynix raises $26.5B in the biggest foreign IPO in US history, is urged to build new US fabs
blurb: SK Hynix raised $26.5B in its Nasdaq debut, the largest-ever US IPO by a non-American company, topping Alibaba's 2014 offering. Demand ran more than seven times the shares on offer and the stock opened 14% above its price, a rare escape from the Korea Discount that usually caps South Korean valuations. The reason is memory: SK Hynix supplies the high-bandwidth memory inside Nvidia's AI GPUs, and the proceeds fund new fabs, packaging, and EUV scanners. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick is now pressing SK Hynix and Samsung to build those factories on US soil.

title: The Builder’s Economy: 10 Metrics from ICONIQ’s Newest 2026 State of AI Report
blurb: ICONIQ's third State of AI report, built on a Q2 2026 survey of about 305 software executives, marks a shift in thesis: the January edition was about proving AI works, this one is about proving AI pays. AI products went from 32% of revenue in 2025 to a projected 42% this year, with 2027 crossing 53% and becoming the majority of the business. The other reversal is in the stack, as open source climbed to 40% of the app layer and now edges past in-house models, so the moat is moving from whose model you use to who controls the stack.

title: Stablecoin issuer Circle just got the greenlight to operate as a bank. The shares are up 5%
blurb: Circle won an OCC trust bank charter, letting it manage the reserves behind its $73B USDC stablecoin directly instead of relying on third-party banks and custodians. Shares closed up about 5%. The charter swaps 50 state rulebooks for one federal regulator and signals the broader shift crypto firms are making from financial applications to regulated financial infrastructure, with Coinbase, Ripple, Paxos, and BitGo all chasing the same OCC approvals. The catch for Circle is that the GENIUS Act framework also invites traditional banks to issue their own stablecoins, turning today's customers into tomorrow's competitors.

title: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI on Friday for trade secret theft and breach of contract, alleging a pattern of poaching Apple staff to extract confidential details about unannounced hardware. The complaint names Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan, a 24-year Apple veteran, and accuses OpenAI of coaching departing employees to evade security and download proprietary design documents. The suit lands as OpenAI is rumored to be building an iPhone competitor with Jony Ive's io team, which means the legal fight is really a proxy for who controls the next hardware platform.

title: US Eases Export Curbs on UAE, Opening Door for AI Chip Sales
blurb: The Trump administration plans to ease export restrictions on the UAE, clearing the Gulf state to buy advanced semiconductors for its AI buildout. The move opens one of the largest sovereign compute markets to US chipmakers and cements the UAE as a serious node in the global AI infrastructure race. For founders, the map of where large-scale training and inference capacity will sit is being redrawn around governments with capital to deploy, not just the incumbent hyperscalers.

title: JPMorgan Builds AI Agents That Beat 60/40 Model in Backtests
blurb: JPMorgan built AI agents that allocate capital directly, and in backtests they beat the classic 60/40 portfolio. The bank is pushing past the current use of AI for stock picking and risk analysis toward letting a model make the allocation call itself. Backtests flatter every strategy, so the real test is live money and drawdowns nobody modeled, but the direction is clear: the largest US bank is treating portfolio construction as something an agent can own, not just assist.

title: The White House Made Fixing Intel Its Pet Project. It’s Working.
blurb: Intel's turnaround is running on political pressure, not just product. The White House has made reviving the chip maker a priority, leaning on Apple, Nvidia, and other potential customers and partners to route business its way, and the company's numbers are starting to improve. For founders, it's a reminder that in strategic sectors like semiconductors, industrial policy now shapes who wins as much as technology or price does, and a supplier's biggest advantage can be which side of the government it sits on.

title: SK Hynix Debut Is a Bet That AI Breaks Boom-and-Bust Chip Cycle
blurb: SK Hynix pulled off the largest US public listing ever by a foreign company, with shares up 13% on day one. The bet underneath the debut is that AI demand for memory chips is structural enough to flatten the boom-and-bust cycle that has defined the industry for decades. If it holds, memory stops being a cyclical commodity play and becomes a durable AI infrastructure position, but the entire thesis rides on data-center buildout staying elevated long after the current capex wave crests.

title: 5 Interesting Learnings from Vertical B2B Leader ServiceTitan at $1B+ in ARR.  Not Slowing Down, Growing 25%, Fintech Growing Fastest, 110% NRR
blurb: ServiceTitan crossed a $1B revenue run rate the hard way, growing 25% to $268.8M in the quarter while doubling its non-GAAP operating margin to 15.2% and clearing Rule of 40. The real engine is fintech, not seats: $21.7B in gross transaction volume flowed through the platform, and usage revenue grew 29% versus 24% for subscriptions, now making up about 22% of platform revenue. Yet the stock trades at roughly 7x trailing revenue, down 40% from its high, which tells you how the market is pricing durable vertical B2B in 2026: consistency isn't enough when buyers want acceleration.

title: Ways to think about token pricing
blurb: Foundation model pricing is unstable, and every dynamic Evans can see points toward the labs becoming low-margin commodity infrastructure rather than pricing-power businesses. Inference runs at 40-50% gross margins today, but that excludes training the next model twice a year, which still costs far more than revenue. The first-half supply crunch came from product-market fit in exactly one use case, software development, so nobody knows what the next scaled workload needs or what anyone will pay for it.

title: Lovable is Reportedly in Talks to Raise $300M at a $13.2bn Valuation
blurb: Lovable is reportedly in talks to raise $300M at a $13.2B valuation, per Sifted, roughly double the $6.6B it commanded at its Series B in December. The Swedish vibe-coding startup has passed $500M in annualized revenue with just 146 staff and about a million new projects starting each week, one of the steepest software growth curves on record. The round is still under discussion, and the figure rides an AI-funding wave with vibe-coding security concerns unresolved, as rivals like Base44 build their own models to compete.

title: Meta Ordered by E.U. to Alter ‘Addictive Design’ of Instagram and Facebook
blurb: EU regulators ordered Meta to change the "addictive design" of Instagram and Facebook, ruling that features like infinite scroll and engagement-optimized feeds violate the bloc's digital safety law.
