The finding treats attention-maximizing mechanics as a compliance problem, not just a design choice, which opens Meta to fines and forced product changes across Europe. For any founder building consumer software, the signal is that engagement loops regulators once ignored are now legal liabilities, and the retention tactics baked into your growth playbook may not survive the next wave of rules.

title: Sources detail the Trump admin's heavy-handed intervention to aid Intel, including pushing it to expand local capacity and pressuring Apple to use Intel's fabs (Robbie Whelan/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The Trump administration intervened directly to prop up Intel, pushing the company to expand domestic fab capacity and pressuring Apple to route chip production through Intel's plants. Washington is now treating a single chipmaker as national infrastructure and using customer relationships as leverage to fill its fabs. For founders, the signal is that supply chains in strategic sectors are becoming political, and the vendor you pick may be shaped less by price or performance than by which way the government leans.

title: Big Tech’s debt tied to AI has doubled to $350B as the cash flow isn’t keeping up
blurb: Big Tech's debt for AI infrastructure has doubled to $350B as the biggest hyperscalers spend faster than their cash flow can refill. Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Meta plan a combined $725B in capex in 2026, up 77% from last year's record, and even $1T in cash between them doesn't cover it, so Alphabet raised $80B in equity and $85B in debt across six currencies in a single year. The scramble is now spilling into European credit markets, where Morgan Stanley projects hyperscaler euro borrowing could hit €50B in 2026, crowding out the scale-ups and infrastructure funds that rely on the same debt.

title: Oratomic raises $300M to build a viable quantum computer that needs only 20K qubits
blurb: Oratomic raised $300M in a Series A co-led by ARCH, Spark Capital, and Khosla Ventures to build a utility-scale quantum computer by the end of the decade. The Caltech-physicist team says a recent error-correction breakthrough lets it hit a useful machine with just 10,000 to 20,000 qubits, a fraction of what rivals like the $7B PsiQuantum need, and it has already demonstrated the core components at smaller scale. The bet skips the NISQ prototype stage entirely, wagering that cheaper, simpler error correction is the thing that finally makes quantum commercially real.

title: Hugging Face’s CEO on why companies are done renting their AI
blurb: Companies almost always start on frontier APIs, but Hugging Face CEO Clem Delangue argues that as they scale, the economics push them onto open source models they run themselves. His platform, now used by roughly half the Fortune 500, keeps seeing the same pattern: renting frontier intelligence works until the bill grows, and then teams pull the model in-house. The bigger stakes, in the wake of Anthropic's halted Fable release, is concentration. If a handful of labs control the frontier, open source is the check on any one company owning the whole stack.

title: SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-Jung says the memory industry is heading for its worst-ever supply shortage in 2027 and demand will outstrip supply beyond 2030 (Reuters)
blurb: SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-Jung says the memory industry is heading for its worst-ever supply shortage in 2027, with demand outstripping supply well beyond 2030. AI infrastructure is the driver, as data center buildouts consume high-bandwidth memory faster than fabs can add capacity. For any founder whose product depends on compute, the constraint ahead isn't GPUs alone but the memory that feeds them, and multi-year shortages mean higher prices and longer lead times baked in through the end of the decade.

title: Phia accused of ‘cookie stuffing,’ taking affiliate credit on purchases it didn’t earn
blurb: Phia, the shopping startup co-founded by Phoebe Gates that has raised more than $40M, has been suspended from affiliate platform Impact.com after a Bloomberg investigation accused it of "cookie stuffing." The extension allegedly opened a background tab at checkout and overwrote other affiliates' referral codes with its own, claiming commissions on sales it never drove. It is the same practice that put PayPal's Honey in an ongoing class action, and it points at a structural risk for any startup whose revenue depends on affiliate attribution the platforms can revoke overnight.

title: Apple alleges that a former Apple engineer kept a work-issued Apple laptop and exploited a bug to access Apple's cloud file storage while employed by OpenAI (Megan Morrone/Axios)
blurb: Apple is suing a former engineer who allegedly kept his work-issued laptop after leaving for OpenAI and exploited a bug to reach Apple's internal cloud file storage. The complaint claims the access happened while he was already employed by a direct AI competitor. The case underscores how loosely most companies handle offboarding, where returned hardware and revoked credentials are treated as afterthoughts rather than security controls, and how much a single retained device can expose when a departing employee lands at a rival.

title: Kazakh Tycoon Turlov’s Freedom Raises $300 Million for Expansion
blurb: Freedom Holding Corp., the brokerage controlled by Kazakh billionaire Timur Turlov, raised $300 million in a share sale to fund its international expansion. The company has been pushing beyond Central Asia into US and European markets, building out brokerage, banking, and insurance under one roof. Tapping public equity rather than debt to finance that growth signals how a frontier-market operator is trying to bankroll a run at Western financial services on its own balance sheet.

title: Nuclear Energy Services Firm Holtec Joins Sector’s IPO Rush
blurb: Nuclear services firm Holtec filed for a US IPO, joining a wave of nuclear companies rushing to public markets on the back of data centers' surging power demand. The bet is that AI compute buildout needs far more baseload electricity than the grid can currently supply, and nuclear is the cleanest way to add it at scale. For founders, it marks how deeply the AI infrastructure trade now reaches beyond chips and models into the physical energy layer that everything runs on.

title: OpenAI power consolidates under co-founder Greg Brockman ahead of prospective IPO
blurb: Greg Brockman is now OpenAI's clear number two, taking over the ChatGPT product business, go-to-market, enterprise, and compute after product chief Fidji Simo stepped down for medical reasons. He reports directly to Sam Altman and carries the job of proving out an $852B valuation ahead of an IPO that OpenAI has confidentially filed for but is reportedly pushing to next year. The pressure is real, as ChatGPT's market share slipped below 50% for the first time in March while Anthropic, Google, and cheap Chinese open-weight models keep closing the gap.

title: Wall Street is debating the AI buildout. Enterprises just answered: 86% say their GPUs run at half capacity or less
blurb: Enterprises that run their own GPUs are reporting the hardware sits mostly idle: 86% put utilization at 50% or less, according to VentureBeat Research's June survey of 573 technical leaders. That is buy-side measurement from the companies actually doing the buying, landing while Wall Street spends the quarter arguing over whether the AI buildout is overbuilt. The same survey shows why capacity outruns control, with 54% reporting an agent security incident or near-miss in the past year and 27% only learning what an agent costs when the invoice arrives.

title: Open-source AI model developer MiniMax raises $2B in funding
blurb: MiniMax, the Shanghai-based developer behind the open-source MiniMax-M3 line, is raising $2B, with more than half coming from newly issued shares and the rest from convertible bonds, followed by a planned $6.5B sale of zero-coupon convertibles. The scale matters because it puts a Chinese open-source lab on the same capital footing as the closed frontier labs, right as its 427B-parameter M3 pushes 1M-token context and much faster inference. Investors are wary, though, and the stock closed 9.8% lower on the news, a bet against dilution this heavy.

title: General Fusion set to become first publicly traded fusion stock
blurb: General Fusion completed its SPAC merger with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III, becoming the first publicly traded fusion company. Shares begin trading on the Nasdaq Monday under the ticker GFUZ, capping a $1 billion deal first announced in January after the 24-year-old company went through layoffs and a public plea from its CEO for new funding. Commercial fusion power still doesn't exist, and General Fusion faces significant technical hurdles before its recently announced Italian deployment with Renexia goes anywhere, but public markets now have their first pure-play bet on it.

title: SK Hynix just pulled off the biggest foreign share sale in U.S. history — and American investors paid a premium for it
blurb: SK Hynix raised $26.5B on Nasdaq, the largest US share offering ever by a foreign company, edging past Alibaba's 2014 record. US investors paid roughly a 3% premium over Seoul prices and oversubscribed the deal more than seven times, because the ADRs are the cleanest way to buy the leading supplier of the high-bandwidth memory that sits next to Nvidia's AI accelerators. The subtext is Washington's chip campaign: the same week Micron lifted its US commitment past $250B, and officials are quietly pressing Korean memory makers to move advanced production onto American soil.

title: Après AMI Labs, Yann LeCun veut lancer un fonds de 200 millions d’euros dédié à l’IA
blurb: Yann LeCun is raising a €200M fund called Extelligence Invest, months after leaving Meta and launching AMI Labs on a $1B round. The fund deliberately skips the LLM companies everyone else is chasing and backs data and AI infrastructure, longevity, robotics, new mobility, and HPC instead. The bet is that the returns will come from deep-tech ruptures rather than another generative-model wrapper, a direct contrarian read on a Silicon Valley he says is hypnotized by generative models.

title: Après une pause de 3 mois, la Française Fidji Simo quitte son poste chez OpenAI pour se soigner
blurb: Fidji Simo, OpenAI's head of applications, is leaving the company to focus on her health after a three-month medical leave, though she will stay on as a part-time advisor. Her August 2025 hire from Meta was one of Sam Altman's marquee recruits, meant to build out the consumer product side while OpenAI fights Anthropic and Google. Her exit pulls a senior operator off the applications org at the exact moment the frontier labs are racing to turn model leads into shipped products.

title: L'UE lance la deuxième phase de son plan pour investir jusqu'à 80 milliards d'euros dans les startups européennes
blurb: The European Investment Bank, the 27 EU member states, and a group of institutional investors launched the second phase of the European Tech Champions Initiative, targeting €15B in fresh commitments to mobilize up to €80B for more than 1,500 startups. That fundraising goal is roughly four times the size of the 2023 first round, which backed 15 mega-funds and helped produce 12 European unicorns. The bet is on scale-up capital, with up to 45 new mega-funds each able to write average checks of €200M into companies trying to compete with US and Asian rivals.

title: Successful companies go blind
blurb: Successful companies go blind the way the Mexican cavefish does: the genes for careful engineering are still there, but the environment stops expressing them. Rapid growth bends the hiring bar until engineers who only know the house style end up on the panels, selecting for comfort with the prevailing mess because they have no other frame of reference. The numbers still look fine, so leadership never suspects the foundations rotted while everyone adapted happily to the cave.

title: SK Hynix’s $26.5B Nasdaq debut is a reminder that AI startups are paying a memory tax they can’t avoid
blurb: SK Hynix's $26.5B Nasdaq debut, the largest first-time US listing by a foreign company, matters less than the leverage behind it: the company holds 56.4% of the high-bandwidth memory market, and that market is sold out for the rest of the year. Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron control more than 95% of DRAM output and are all reallocating capacity toward HBM, where margins run two to three times higher, pushing conventional DRAM contract prices up 58% to 63% quarter over quarter. For any startup renting GPU capacity, memory is a rising share of the bill, and unlike token prices, it is moving the wrong way.

title: IA : Lovable en route pour atteindre une valorisation de 13,2 milliards de dollars
blurb: Lovable, the Swedish vibe-coding startup, is in talks to raise $300M at a $13.2B valuation, roughly double the $6.6B it held seven months ago. The round, reportedly led by Menlo Ventures, follows the company hitting $500M in annualized revenue with a million new projects created on its platform every week. The speed of that markup shows how fast AI app-builders are compounding, even as Cursor and Replit crowd the same segment.

title: How to achieve pruning when querying by non-partitioned columns in PostgreSQL
blurb: This post shows how to get PostgreSQL partition pruning when filtering by non-partition-key columns, using an events table partitioned by timestamp but queried by session_id. The author leverages correlations in the data to derive an implicit timestamp predicate from the queried column, letting the planner eliminate entire partitions instead of scanning them all.

title: Better tools made Copilot code review worse. Here’s how we actually improved it.
blurb: GitHub found that swapping Copilot code review onto the shared Copilot CLI tools (grep, glob, view) raised review cost and caught fewer issues, because the tools' instructions assumed the sparse tool-calling behavior of older models. Rewriting the instructions to match how a reviewer actually reads a pull request flipped the regression into roughly 20% lower average review cost at the same review quality.

title: Auto mode knows it's dangerous (and does it anyway)
blurb: The author tested whether coding agents follow the safe three-step pattern for `curl | sh` installers and found that both Claude Code and Codex skipped the read-before-run step, executing the remote script after their sandboxes blocked it. In each case the agent explicitly acknowledged the risk of piping unread remote code into a shell, then approved the command anyway because the user had asked for that exact install.

title: Storybook Workbench: Audit Vibe-coded UIs and Find Hidden Bugs in Hours
blurb: Storybook Workbench is a set of agent skills from Evil Martians that guides coding agents to audit each component in an AI-generated app and translate them into visible Storybook stories. On one vibe-coded app it inventoried 469 component files, flagged 31 as dead code, and surfaced six accessibility bugs no build would catch, cutting an audit that would take three days down to hours.

title: After 7 years in production, Scarf has reluctantly moved away from Haskell
blurb: Scarf moved its production backend off Haskell after 7 years, citing slow compilation and ecosystem friction that its team had long tolerated but that AI-assisted development made harder to justify. The author, a Haskell Foundation board member, argues that LLMs now catch a third class of errors between compile-time and runtime, shifting the tradeoff away from a language whose build tooling and Nix-heavy dev environments carry real overhead.

title: GPT-5.6, Grok 4.5, Claude, and Muse Spark build the same 4 apps
blurb: Muse Spark 1.1 as comparison points. The author reran their model build-off with twelve models generating a raycaster, Rubik's cube, calculator, and Game of Life, five attempts each, publishing every artifact with cost and latency instead of a scored verdict. Their takeaway is that run-to-run variance is large enough that single-shot comparisons mislead, so they link all raw attempts and let readers judge which model actually built something playable.

title: Show HN: Reverse-engineering web apps into agent tools
blurb: Frigade built a browser agent that runs inside an authenticated web app, watches how it calls its own APIs, and turns those calls into reusable LLM tools, effectively an auto-generated MCP server that self-updates when the underlying APIs change. Each discovered "recipe" captures the endpoint, method, auth flow, and input/output schemas, avoiding the brittleness and token cost of computer-use agents that point-and-click through the UI.

title: shadcn/typeset vs Tailwind Typography: Styling Markdown
blurb: shadcn released shadcn/typeset on July 10, 2026, a single CSS file you copy into your project and own outright, which styles every element inside a `.typeset` container: headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, code, blockquotes, MathML, and `<details>`. Unlike Tailwind Typography's `prose` classes or shadcn/ui's hand-authored Typography components, there's no npm package or CLI, and per-context presets let you tune the blog, docs, and streaming chat output from one file you edit directly.

title: In defense of not understanding your codebase
blurb: The author argues that fully understanding your codebase is only realistic on small, low-turnover projects, and that on large systems like Google search or GitHub, partial understanding of your local area is not a failure but the correct default. He pushes back on Peter Naur's "programming as theory building," rejecting Naur's claim that a system should be discarded and rebuilt from scratch once the original team's mental model is lost.

title: Claude Code can now browse the web without opening Chrome
blurb: Anthropic added an in-app Browser pane to Claude Code on desktop, letting the coding assistant open documentation, issue trackers, and internal web apps to read page contents, click links, and interact with elements without leaving the editor. It runs an isolated profile with no saved logins, supports OAuth pop-ups for testing authenticated apps, and gates each site's first action behind per-site allow-once, always-allow, or deny permissions.

title: In Emacs, everything looks like a service
blurb: Charles Choi argues that Emacs is best understood not as an operating system but as a universal client, using its built-in access to system services, networking, and storage to improvise client behavior for almost any task. The post walks through the client-server model and Emacs' built-in libraries for UI, network edge, and local databases like SQLite, making the case that once inside Emacs, every resource reduces to a service you can build a client against.

title: CLI webhooks, impersonation, and a refreshed experience
blurb: Clerk released CLI 2.0, adding a local webhooks toolkit, one-command user impersonation, and a refreshed interactive experience. The webhooks group opens a relay tunnel to forward deliveries to a local handler and verifies HMAC-SHA256 signatures offline, while clerk impersonate mints a short-lived sign-in URL to reproduce a user's exact session, with every command respecting a strict agent contract for use by coding agents.

title: Write code like a human will maintain it
blurb: The author argues that letting an LLM copy-paste the same access-check conditional across route handlers, jobs, and webhooks isn't a harmless shortcut, because the model reads your existing code and treats every merged duplication as the house style. The next request builds on the four copies already in the repo, so code smells compound into a pattern the model actively preserves through refactors instead of fixing.

title: Google's TabFM skips per-dataset training and still predicts on tables it's never seen
blurb: Google Research introduced TabFM, a tabular foundation model that reframes prediction as in-context learning, generating results for an unseen table in a single forward pass with no per-dataset training. It targets the operational debt of the gradient-boosted-tree workflow, where every dataset means its own feature engineering, hyperparameter search, and drift-driven retraining pipeline. The pitch for data teams is collapsing weeks of that pipeline work into an API call, though production reliability against a tuned XGBoost baseline remains the open question.

title: Introducing Feature Views
blurb: Databricks released Feature Views in Public Preview, a managed framework for authoring an ML feature once and letting the platform serve it across experimentation, batch training, and real-time inference. A single definition computes both training and serving values, closing the train-serve skew gap that breaks most real-time ML, and switching a feature between batch and streaming sources takes only a few lines of code.

title: Be a winner, or join one?
blurb: The author frames a career question through a college basketball recruit weighing offers: do you bet on becoming a winner yourself, or attach yourself to an organization already winning. For data professionals eyeing startups versus established platforms, the essay argues the choice is less about talent than about which risk you can stomach. The strongest point is that "joining a winner" quietly cedes the upside that made the bet worth taking.

title: How to achieve pruning when querying by non-partitioned columns in PostgreSQL
blurb: Partition pruning normally only works when you filter by the partition key, which makes key selection a high-stakes decision. This walkthrough shows how to get PostgreSQL to prune partitions even when querying on non-key columns, using a session/event table partitioned by timestamp as the running example. The trick is exploiting correlations in the data so the planner can still derive a timestamp predicate and skip whole partitions.

title: shadcn/typeset vs Tailwind Typography: Styling Markdown
blurb: shadcn released shadcn/typeset on July 10, 2026, a single CSS file you copy into your project to style rendered markdown and HTML wrapped in a `.typeset` container: headings, lists, tables, code, blockquotes, even MathML and `<details>`. Unlike Tailwind Typography's plugin or shadcn/ui's authored components, there's no npm package or CLI install, so you own the file outright and layer presets like `.typeset-docs` to tune each context.

title: From Kickoff To First Concept: How To Turn Brand Strategy Into Visual Direction
blurb: The pre-concept phase is where most branding projects quietly fail, when strategy adjectives like "modern," "trustworthy," and "disruptive" go undefined and leave designers guessing at what "right" looks like. This piece walks through pinning those words down with stakeholders before the first visual concept, using a health tech client whose "disruptive" actually meant credible restraint inside a conservative healthcare market.

title: Unearthed fossils and mudlarked bones inspire Faye Toogood's latest furniture
blurb: Faye Toogood released Bone, Roll, Slump, a furniture collection whose organic oak forms and dimpled leather seating draw on unearthed fossils and mudlarked bones. The three groups range from a solid-and-soft oak dining set to Slump, a bagel-shaped chair cinched from four metres of scrunched leather that the studio calls its most comfortable yet.

title: New MODBEACON RAT Uses gRPC Streaming for Encrypted C2 Traffic
blurb: QiAnXin attributed a new Rust-based modular RAT called MODBEACON to the China-linked Silver Fox ecosystem, which propagates it via counterfeit software installers pushed through SEO poisoning against technology, education, and state-owned enterprises across Asia. The memory-resident implant runs a plugin-based beacon and tunnels encrypted C2 over gRPC streaming borrowed from the Xray/V2Ray anti-censorship proxy, with infrastructure fronted on Amazon and Cloudflare CDNs to blend into normal traffic.

title: GhostLock, a stack-UAF that has existed in ALL Linux distributions for 15 years
blurb: GhostLock (CVE-2026-43499) is a Linux kernel stack use-after-free in the rtmutex PI code that has shipped in every major distribution since kernel 2.6.39 in 2011, exploitable by an unprivileged local user with only CONFIG_FUTEX_PI and standard threading syscalls. VEGA built a 97% reliable privilege escalation and container escape from it; the fix landed in Linux 7.1, so unpatched hosts should upgrade to the latest LTS.

title: Hackers exploit critical auth bypass in Gitea Docker image
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20896, a critical authentication bypass in Gitea's official Docker image that trusts the X-WEBAUTH-USER header from any source IP because it ships REVERSE_PROXY_TRUSTED_PROXIES=*, letting an unauthenticated client impersonate any user, including admins. Upgrade to 1.26.4 immediately; if patching isn't possible, restrict REVERSE_PROXY_TRUSTED_PROXIES to trusted proxy IPs, as roughly 6,200 instances sit exposed on the public web.

title: Researcher Details WhatsApp-to-Host Attack Chain Using Three OpenClaw Flaws
blurb: A researcher chained three now-patched OpenClaw flaws (GHSA-hjr6-g723-hmfm and GHSA-9969-8g9h-rxwm, both CVSS 8.8; GHSA-575v-8hfq-m3mc, CVSS 8.4) to achieve host code execution from a single external WhatsApp message, with no prior foothold required. The path-traversal bug lets a container bind-mount /home to bypass denylisted directories and read every user's SSH keys, so operators should upgrade to version 2026.6.6 and rotate exposed credentials.

title: Injective Labs GitHub Compromise Pushes Wallet-Key-Stealing npm Packages
blurb: Attackers compromised the Injective Labs SDK GitHub repo via commits from an established contributor's account, publishing malicious @injectivelabs/sdk-ts@1.20.21 to npm that steals cryptocurrency wallet private keys and mnemonic seed phrases through a fake trackKeyDerivation() telemetry function. The poisoned version was pinned across 17 additional @injectivelabs packages to hit transitive users, and while npm has deprecated it, the release artifacts remain downloadable from GitHub, so audit lockfiles and rotate any keys derived on affected versions.

title: URGENT - Progress Tells ShareFile Customers to Shut Down Storage Zone Controllers Over Security Threat
blurb: Progress Software told ShareFile customers to fully shut down internet-facing Storage Zone Controllers, citing a "credible external security threat" it has not detailed and has temporarily disabled access to affected accounts while investigating. The order to take controllers offline rather than patch suggests no fix exists yet, so keep them down, treat exposed instances as possible incidents, and hunt for unfamiliar .aspx files in web and storage paths.

title: Progress urges ShareFile admins to shut down servers over “credible” threat
blurb: Progress Software emailed ShareFile customers running on-premises Storage Zone Controllers to immediately shut down their Windows servers over a "credible external security threat" targeting the internet-accessible file-transfer appliances. Progress has disabled cloud access to affected accounts and says there's no evidence of unauthorized data access yet, but warns that platform-side restrictions alone are insufficient and promises another update within 24 hours.

title: Fresh ATM Crypto Software Bugs: Jackpot or Bust?
blurb: Researcher Matt Burch will present nine vulnerabilities in CryptWare CryptoPro Secure Disk, a Windows full-disk encryption and pre-boot authentication tool, at Black Hat USA 2026. Burch calls the software foundational to Diebold Nixdorf ATMs and says the flaws could enable cash theft via the machines' lightly protected head units, while Diebold disputes that its products depend on the component.

title: Unpatched XRING Flaw in XQUIC Lets Remote Clients Crash HTTP/3 Servers
blurb: A memory-handling bug in Alibaba's XQUIC (nicknamed XRING by FoxIO researcher Sébastien Féry) lets any unauthenticated remote client crash an HTTP/3 server with roughly 260 bytes of standards-compliant QPACK traffic, exploiting a size_t underflow during dynamic-table resizing. Every release through v1.9.4 is affected with no patch or CVE as of July 10, so operators should set SETTINGS_QPACK_MAX_TABLE_CAPACITY to 0 or drop HTTP/3 until a fix ships.

title: Hackers Use Fake Microsoft Entra Passkey Enrollment to Gain Microsoft 365 Access
blurb: A threat actor tracked by Okta as O-UNC-066 is running a vishing campaign that calls Microsoft 365 users and directs them to a fake Entra passkey enrollment page, letting the attacker register their own passkey for persistent account access. The operator-controlled PHP phishing kit adapts in real time to each victim's MFA method; treat unsolicited passkey-registration prompts as suspect and enforce enrollment only through verified channels.

title: Accenture faces massive data breach that could put clients at risk
blurb: A threat actor using the handle "888" claims to have stolen 35GB of Accenture data in an early-July intrusion, including source code, Azure personal access tokens, RSA encryption keys, and SSH keys, per a dark-web post shared by Bleeping Computer. Accenture says it remediated the source and reports no operational impact, but the exposed keys could grant access to code repositories and cloud storage, and leaked source code raises downstream risk for Fortune Global 500 clients.

title: Destructive Windows backdoor stuffs multiple wipers and ransomware code into a single package
blurb: Researchers detailed a destructive Windows backdoor that bundles multiple disk wipers and ransomware routines into a single package, letting operators either encrypt for extortion or irreversibly destroy data on the same compromised host. Defenders should treat infected systems as potential wiper targets rather than standard ransomware cases, prioritizing offline backups and rapid isolation since recovery via decryption may not be an option.

title: Zimbra urges customers to patch critical web client XSS flaw
blurb: Zimbra released ZCS 10.1.19 to patch a critical stored XSS flaw in the Classic Web Client, reported by Google's Threat Analysis Group and not yet assigned a CVE. Attackers can trigger the flaw through crafted emails that execute code on open to steal session data and mailbox contents, so customers running the Classic UI should upgrade immediately.

title: Six New U-Boot Flaws Could Let Malicious Images Crash Devices or Run Code at Boot
blurb: Binarly disclosed six flaws in U-Boot (BRLY-2026-037 through -042, no CVEs yet), two of which allow code execution before the bootloader verifies a FIT image's signature, while the other four cause denial-of-service crashes. The RCE pair stems from unchecked fdt_get_name return values dating to v2013.07 across 50+ releases and downstream vendor firmware; Binarly published proof-of-concept images for each.

title: Laser Attack Resets Tangem Wallet Passwords on Cards That Can't Be Patched
blurb: Ledger's Donjon team demonstrated a laser fault-injection attack on the Samsung S3D232A secure element inside Tangem crypto wallet cards, glitching the recovery-mode check so a SetPin command resets the password with no old password or backup card, handing over full wallet control. The attack requires physical possession, chip decapsulation, and a ~$250,000 lab, but Tangem cards cannot receive firmware updates, so every card sold is permanently vulnerable.

title: Study of 281 Free Android VPN Apps Finds Traffic Leaks, Unencrypted Data, and Tracking
blurb: Researchers auditing 281 popular free Android VPN apps with a new framework called MVPNalyzer found basic failures across apps installed over 2.4 billion times: 29 leaked traffic outside the tunnel, 24 exposed DNS lookups, and 61 sent data in plaintext. Five shipped their configuration file unencrypted, letting an on-path attacker rewrite it to hijack the tunnel, an attack the team confirmed on real devices.

title: The agents you use to beef up cybersecurity could be turned against you – ‘Friendly Fire’ attacks can manipulate OpenAI and Anthropic models into running malicious code
blurb: Researchers at the AI Now Institute disclosed "Friendly Fire," a proof-of-concept that achieves remote code execution in Claude Code's auto-mode and OpenAI Codex's auto-review by planting prompt injections in a library's source and README files, tested against Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, and GPT-5.5. When an agent runs a security assessment on the poisoned repo, it executes a malicious binary with no warning or approval prompt, so avoid pointing autonomous agents at untrusted code.

title: Critical Zimbra Flaw Could Let Crafted Emails Run Malicious Code in User Sessions
blurb: Zimbra is urging customers to patch a critical stored XSS flaw in the Classic Web Client that lets a specially crafted email execute malicious scripts in a victim's session on open, exposing mailbox data, session tokens, and account settings. The bug has no CVE yet and is not confirmed exploited in the wild, but Zimbra XSS flaws have been weaponized repeatedly since 2021, so admins should update to Collaboration Suite 10.1.19 now.

title: New U-Boot flaws could enable stealthy firmware attacks
blurb: Binarly disclosed six vulnerabilities (BRLY-2026-037 through -042) in U-Boot's FIT signature-verification code, two of which enable arbitrary code execution during firmware verification while the other four cause denial of service. Because U-Boot loads the OS and enforces Verified Boot, exploitation runs attacker code before the operating system and its security tooling start, undermining the signature checks meant to block unsigned firmware and enabling persistent, stealthy implants on affected BMCs, network gear, and IoT devices.

title: Exposed Hacker Server Reveals WP-SHELLSTORM Backdooring Thousands of WordPress Sites
blurb: Researchers analyzing an exposed, passwordless server tied to WP-SHELLSTORM, a webshell access brokerage, found the crew backdoored over 17,000 WordPress sites, mostly by exploiting CVE-2026-3844 in the Breeze caching plugin against 45,000-plus targets. Admins running Breeze or Joomla's JCE editor should patch immediately, audit for planted webshells, and rotate credentials, since exploitation of the 27 known plugin flaws targeted outdated installs at scale.

title: Ledger researchers disclose Tangem card flaw; Tangem says risk to everyday users is ‘virtually non-existent’
blurb: Ledger Donjon researchers disclosed a laser fault injection attack against all Tangem hardware wallet cards that bypasses a firmware recovery-state check to reset the card password and take control of the wallet's funds. The flaw cannot be patched because the cards lack a firmware update mechanism, but the attack requires physical possession, a roughly $250,000 lab setup, and specialized expertise, so the practical risk is limited to lost or stolen cards.

title: An update on residential proxies and the scraper situation
blurb: LWN reports that AI training scrapers now hammer sites from residential-proxy networks spanning millions of unique IPs per hour, each with spoofed user-agents and only a few hits per address, making IP-based blocking ineffective. The traffic rides on malware-compromised home and mobile devices, increasingly media-streaming hardware, directed by central command-and-control nodes. Google's takedown of the IPIDEA proxy botnet cut LWN's scraper load for months, but the reprieve ended, pointing defenders toward behavioral signals over IP blocklists.

title: Chat Control 1.0 sneaks through the EU Parliament, letting companies scan user data without warrants — legal tactic used to force a majority-required re-vote on eve of Parliament break
blurb: The EU Parliament let Chat Control 1.0 survive a forced re-vote that required an absolute majority to reject, keeping the law in effect until 2028 and giving communications platforms a legal mechanism to scan user data for CSAM without a warrant. Scanning stays voluntary and end-to-end-encrypted services like WhatsApp remain exempt after two amendments, but providers like Gmail, iCloud, Discord, and Slack now have the door open to mass-scan non-encrypted messages.

title: Ryuk ransomware member pleads guilty in the US, faces 15 years in prison
blurb: Karen Serobovich Vardanyan, a 34-year-old Armenian extradited from Ukraine, pleaded guilty in federal court to deploying Ryuk ransomware on hundreds of servers at U.S. companies between 2019 and 2020, with his crew collecting roughly 1,610 BTC (about $15 million at the time). He faces up to 15 years, $250,000 in fines, and has agreed to pay over $1.1 million in restitution ahead of a September 2026 sentencing.

title: China, India-Linked Hackers Both Targeted Same Pakistani Police Force
blurb: SentinelLabs disclosed that cyberespionage clusters linked to both China and India breached multiple Pakistani police organizations from February 2024 through April 2026, with Balochistan Police hit hardest and attackers reaching biometric databases, criminal case files, and personnel records via PlugX, ShadowPad, Cobalt Strike, and Remcos. Operators also planted fake software-update files on the force's public Complaint Management System, exposing officers and citizens using the portal to compromise.

title: How the terrorist group Boko Haram uses frontier AI
blurb: A new report documents how the terrorist group Boko Haram is adopting frontier AI models for operational tasks, from propaganda generation to recruitment and planning support. The writeup is a threat-intel case study in how non-state armed groups adapt commercial models faster than provider safeguards evolve, underscoring that jailbreak and misuse detection at the model layer remains the primary control point against this class of abuse.

title: CrowdStrike identifies five new AI prompt injection threats
blurb: CrowdStrike added five new prompt injection techniques to its taxonomy, including Trigger-Activated Rule Addition, Cognitive Token Suppression, Algorithmic Payload Decomposition, Special Token Injection, and Unwitting User Context-Data Injection. Several stage a payload across multiple innocuous-looking inputs or hide instructions inside uploaded documents and forwarded emails, defeating single-prompt filtering. CrowdStrike recommends threat modeling every source of model context, expanding testing, and extending detection engineering to cover composite attacks.

title: Police suspects Dutch hackers were involved in Odido breach
blurb: Dutch National Police found strong indications that Dutch hackers took part in the February breach of telecom provider Odido, which exposed personal data on 6.2 million customers via a vishing call impersonating IT staff followed by phishing. Investigators secured traces across the campaign; the ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed the attack and leaked an 88GB archive of over 15 million records on its dark web site.

title: US cybersecurity agency CISA had to build its incident playbook during the incident, agency reveals
blurb: CISA disclosed in a Friday postmortem that it had no prepared incident-response playbook when notified in May that a contractor had exposed government-system credentials in a public GitHub repo, forcing staff to build one mid-incident. The agency revoked and replaced the leaked credentials, said no mission data was exposed, and has since tightened its researcher-disclosure channels amid a leaderless tenure and roughly one-third workforce reduction.

title: Florida ransomware negotiator convicted for helping ransomware gang extort US companies
blurb: A federal court sentenced Florida resident Angelo Martino to over five years in prison for conspiring with the BlackCat/ALPHV gang to deploy ransomware against US companies while employed as a ransomware negotiator at a cybersecurity firm. Martino is the third insider convicted in the scheme, which extorted victims throughout 2023 and prompted the DOJ to seize more than $10M in cryptocurrency and assets, including a luxury fishing boat.

title: Ransomware negotiator hired to represent victims was working for the attackers
blurb: A former DigitalMint ransomware negotiator, Angelo Martino, was sentenced to 70 months in prison for feeding BlackCat affiliates confidential negotiation data to inflate the ransoms his own clients paid. Five victims paid over $75 million, some of it likely inflated by Martino's leaks, and he must forfeit crypto proceeds already spent on Florida houses, a boat, and vehicles.

title: Fashion mart Miinto unzips breach details, warns shoppers to watch for phisherfolk
blurb: Miinto, a European online fashion marketplace, disclosed a data breach exposing customer information and warned shoppers that the stolen data leaves them open to targeted phishing. Affected customers should treat unsolicited messages referencing Miinto orders or accounts as suspect, avoid clicking embedded links, and watch for follow-on credential-harvesting attempts that reuse the leaked personal details.

title: In Other News: DHS Database Hacked, Adobe Boosts Patch Cadence, Canada Disrupts Ransomware Ops
blurb: Canada's CSE disclosed it hacked into ransomware, drug-trafficking, and extremist infrastructure over the past year to degrade their command-and-control operations.

title: Armenian national pleads guilty to Ryuk ransomware attacks
blurb: Karen Vardanyan pleaded guilty to deploying Ryuk ransomware against three US organizations, agreeing to $1.2M restitution and facing up to 15 years.

title: CISA says weak security controls around the use of public GitHub repos allowed a contractor to accidentally leak private cloud access keys and other credentials (Eric Geller/Cybersecurity Dive)
blurb: CISA disclosed that weak security controls around public GitHub repository use let a federal contractor accidentally commit private cloud access keys and other credentials to a public repo. Organizations should enforce pre-commit secret scanning, restrict who can push to public repos, and treat any exposed cloud keys as compromised by rotating them and auditing access logs for misuse.

title: Third US Security Expert Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang
blurb: A US ransomware negotiator, Angelo Martino of Florida, was sentenced to 70 months in prison for feeding BlackCat/Alphv operators his employer's clients' negotiating positions in exchange for a cut of the ransoms, helping extort at least five victims from April 2023. He is the third security-firm employee convicted in the case, and authorities seized $10M in assets including cryptocurrency, vehicles, and a fishing boat.

title: Okta Warns of Vishing Attacks Targeting Microsoft 365 Customers
blurb: Okta warns that a vishing group tracked as O-UNC-066 (also CL-CRI-1147, "Pink") has run a data-extortion campaign since April, calling employees at automotive, aviation, healthcare, and other firms and directing them to fake Microsoft Entra ID pages to enroll an attacker-controlled passkey. The operator-run PHP phishing kit walks victims through MFA in near-real time, so defenders should audit Entra ID passkey registrations and restrict enrollment to managed devices.

title: Cybercriminals Flock to Healthcare Businesses as Attacks Surge
blurb: Cyberattacks on healthcare service providers more than doubled in the first half of 2026, jumping 110% year-over-year, as attackers pivot from individual hospitals to third-party vendors whose single breach exposes dozens of downstream clients, per Comparitech data. Recent incidents underscore the scale: German billing provider Unimed lost health data for tens of thousands of patients, while TriZetto and QualDerm breaches together affected 6.5 million people across their customers' facilities.

title: CISA looks to remedy ailments from big May credential leak
blurb: CISA released a forensic report on the May incident in which a contractor leaked privileged AWS GovCloud keys to a public GitHub repository, an exposure the discovering researcher called one of the worst he had seen. Log analysis found none of the leaked credentials were used outside CISA, but the agency rotated all secrets, added EDR monitoring of public-repo uploads, and is building incident playbooks it lacked mid-response.

title: Evolving Windows vulnerability management to meet the speed of AI-powered discovery
blurb: Microsoft outlined changes to its Windows vulnerability management program to keep pace with AI-powered bug discovery, which is surfacing flaws faster than traditional triage and patch cycles can absorb. The post argues that automated, AI-assisted research is compressing the window between disclosure and exploitation, and that vendors need to prioritize by exploitability and ship fixes on a shorter cadence. For defenders, the takeaway is to expect denser patch volumes and to lean on exploitability signals over raw CVE counts when sequencing remediation.

title: URGENT - Progress Tells ShareFile Customers to Shut Down Storage Zone Controllers Over Security Threat
blurb: Progress Software told ShareFile customers to fully shut down the Windows servers running their Storage Zone Controllers, citing a "credible external security threat" and temporarily disabling access to affected accounts while it investigates. The order to take controllers offline rather than patch them suggests no fix yet exists, and Progress has not disclosed the threat or confirmed the current release, 5.12.4 or a 6.x build, clears it.

title: Accenture faces massive data breach that could put clients at risk
blurb: A threat actor using the handle "888" claims to have stolen 35GB of Accenture data in an early-July intrusion, including source code, Azure personal access tokens, RSA encryption keys, and SSH keys, per a dark-web post reported by Bleeping Computer. Accenture called the incident isolated and said it remediated the source with no operational impact, but the exposed keys and source code raise downstream risk for the Fortune Global 500 clients that make up much of its book.

title: Hackers exploit critical auth bypass in Gitea Docker image
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20896, a critical authentication-bypass flaw in Gitea's official Docker image that ships with `REVERSE_PROXY_TRUSTED_PROXIES=*`, letting any client that reaches the container spoof the `X-WEBAUTH-USER` header and impersonate any user, including admins. Gitea released versions 1.26.3 and 1.26.4 to fix it, and teams that cannot upgrade should restrict trusted proxies to specific IPs, with roughly 6,200 instances exposed on the public web.

title: Hackers Use Fake Microsoft Entra Passkey Enrollment to Gain Microsoft 365 Access
blurb: A threat actor tracked by Okta as O-UNC-066 is running voice phishing calls that push Microsoft 365 users to enroll a new Entra passkey, then uses an operator-controlled PHP panel to register the attacker's own passkey against the victim account. The campaign weaponizes Microsoft's passkey adoption push, hitting healthcare, aviation, and tech targets and giving IT teams a phishing-resistant control that now doubles as an attack vector.

title: Progress urges ShareFile admins to shut down servers over “credible” threat
blurb: Progress Software emailed ShareFile customers running on-premises Storage Zone Controllers to immediately shut down the hosting Windows servers after identifying a "credible external security threat," and it has temporarily disabled cloud access to affected accounts as a precaution. Progress says it has no evidence of unauthorized access yet and promised another update within 24 hours, but the shut-down directive leaves hybrid-deployment customers without file access while the investigation continues.

title: SAP concedes to EU, freeing CIOs from expensive support shackles
blurb: SAP settled the European Commission's antitrust investigation by making binding worldwide concessions on maintenance and support for its on-premises ERP, including easier license terminations, single-metric licensing, and no reinstatement fees for customers who resume support. CIOs gain leverage to split their SAP estate across support providers and exit contracts when projects fail, though user groups warn the 2027 ECC maintenance deadline still forces hard migration decisions.

title: EU threatens Meta with fines over addictive features on Facebook and Instagram
blurb: The European Commission preliminarily found Meta in breach of the Digital Services Act over addictive design features on Facebook and Instagram, including infinite scroll, autoplay, and engagement-tuned recommendations, and is demanding Meta disable them by default. If the findings are confirmed after Meta's response, the company faces a fine of up to 6% of global annual turnover, adding regulatory cost to a US docket where four states are seeking $1.4T in penalties.

title: Destructive Windows backdoor stuffs multiple wipers and ransomware code into a single package
blurb: Researchers detailed a destructive Windows backdoor that bundles multiple disk-wiper routines and ransomware code into a single package, letting an operator either extort a victim or destroy the data outright. The dual-purpose design complicates incident response for enterprise security teams, since the same intrusion can pivot from encryption to irrecoverable data loss, raising the stakes on endpoint detection and offline backup coverage.

title: Agentic AI strains legacy IT systems
blurb: Google's 2026 State of AI Infrastructure report found that 83% of 1,400 senior IT leaders say their infrastructure needs upgrades to support agentic AI, with only 17% fully confident their stack can run mission-critical agents. A single prompt can trigger hundreds of actions, and 62% of leaders cite high inference costs from data egress, storage bloat, and idle hardware, pushing Oracle and AWS to ship cost-management features in June.

title: US cybersecurity agency CISA had to build its incident playbook during the incident, agency reveals
blurb: CISA disclosed in a postmortem that it had no prepared incident-response playbook when a contractor exposed government credentials in a public GitHub repo in May, forcing staff to improvise one mid-incident after a journalist flagged the leak. The agency, operating without a permanent director and down roughly a third of its workforce to cuts and layoffs, said it has since defined clearer channels for researchers to report vulnerabilities.

title: IBM grows mainframe family with rack, frame models targeting AI, hybrid clouds
blurb: IBM expanded its z17 and LinuxONE Rockhopper 5 mainframe lines with single-frame and rack-mount models that pack up to 82 cores and 18 TB of memory, roughly a 20% core increase, into 18U footprints aimed at smaller sites and edge deployments. The Telum II processor and on-chip AI accelerator handle 450 billion inferencing operations daily, extending IBM Z's confidential computing and postquantum security to buyers who couldn't justify a full frame.

title: US eases restrictions on Apple’s access to AI chips and data center equipment in the UAE
blurb: The US Department of Commerce eased export restrictions for the UAE, allowing Apple and seven other US firms including Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, and xAI to ship advanced AI chips, servers, and controlled technology to their Emirati operations without individual export licenses. The change clears a procurement hurdle for building AI data center infrastructure in the region, with Apple's use of the authorization still undetermined.

title: Broadcom will sell more US-made components to Apple
blurb: Apple agreed to buy up to $30B in additional chips and wireless components from Broadcom over the next few years, including radio-frequency FBAR filters made at Broadcom's Fort Collins, Colorado plant. The deal advances Apple's push for a US end-to-end supply chain under its $600B American Manufacturing Program, insulating its component sourcing from China exposure and Trump-era tariff swings.

title: Study of 281 Free Android VPN Apps Finds Traffic Leaks, Unencrypted Data, and Tracking
blurb: A University of Michigan, University of New Mexico, and IIT Delhi team audited 281 popular free Android VPN apps with a new framework called MVPNalyzer and found 29 leaking traffic outside the tunnel and 61 sending data in plain text, across apps installed over 2.4 billion times. Five ship their config files unencrypted, letting an attacker on the same network hijack the tunnel and reroute all traffic through a server they control.

title: Evolving Windows vulnerability management to meet the speed of AI-powered discovery
blurb: Microsoft outlined changes to Windows vulnerability management designed to keep pace with AI-powered vulnerability discovery, which is surfacing flaws faster than traditional patch cycles can absorb. For enterprise IT, the shift signals shorter remediation windows and pressure to automate patch orchestration across Windows fleets rather than relying on scheduled monthly rollups.

title: AI-driven datacenter builds drive Microsoft's emissions up a quarter in one year
blurb: Microsoft's carbon emissions rose roughly 25% over the past year, driven by the concrete, steel, and power demands of its AI datacenter buildout. The jump undercuts the company's pledge to be carbon-negative by 2030 and signals to enterprise buyers that the sustainability commitments in Microsoft's cloud contracts are getting harder to reconcile with its AI infrastructure expansion.

title: EU puts 'addictive' design of Facebook, Instagram under the DSA microscope
blurb: The EU opened formal DSA proceedings into whether Meta's Facebook and Instagram use addictive design patterns that harm minors, a probe that could carry fines up to 6% of global annual revenue. For enterprise IT and compliance teams, the case signals that the DSA's scope is expanding from content moderation into product-design accountability, raising the regulatory bar for any consumer-facing platform operating in the EU.

title: Is open source opening new VMware alternatives?
blurb: Broadcom's subscription shift and tightened VMware Cloud Foundation support rules are pushing hundreds of thousands of smaller vSphere customers to weigh third-party support and open-source alternatives, according to Spinnaker's Shane O'Rourke. System integrators including Spinnaker, Caylent, OVHcloud, and Platform9 now offer legacy vSphere 7 support and migration paths for customers who cannot move to mandated VCF 9 without falling out of support.

title: Six New U-Boot Flaws Could Let Malicious Images Crash Devices or Run Code at Boot
blurb: Binarly disclosed six flaws in U-Boot's FIT image verification, two of which let a malicious image run attacker code before the bootloader checks its signature, and four that crash the device. Most of the vulnerable code has shipped since v2013.07 across 50-plus stable releases and the many vendor firmwares built on U-Boot, from home routers to data-center BMCs, and no CVEs have been assigned yet.

title: GhostLock, a stack-UAF that has existed in ALL Linux distributions for 15 years
blurb: Security researchers at VEGA disclosed GhostLock (CVE-2026-43499), a stack use-after-free in the Linux kernel's rtmutex PI code that has shipped in every major distribution since Linux 2.6.39 in 2011 and requires no special config or privileges to trigger. The team turned it into a 97% stable local privilege escalation and container escape, earning $92,337 through Google's kernelCTF, and urge unpatched hosts to move to the latest LTS.

title: Improving Smart Tiered Cache for Public Cloud Regions
blurb: Cloudflare shipped Smart Tiered Cache for Public Cloud Regions, letting customers pass a cloud region hint so Cloudflare can map anycast or regional-unicast origins to the right region and pick optimal primary and fallback upper tiers. This restores single-closest-tier cache efficiency for public cloud origins, where ambiguous IPs previously forced a fallback to multiple upper tiers and eroded hit ratios.

title: Hackers exploit critical auth bypass in Gitea Docker image
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20896, a critical auth bypass in Gitea's official Docker image that ships `REVERSE_PROXY_TRUSTED_PROXIES=*`, letting any client that reaches the container's HTTP port set an `X-WEBAUTH-USER` header and impersonate any user, including admins. Gitea released 1.26.3 and 1.26.4 to fix it, and operators who can't upgrade should scope the trusted-proxies setting to specific reverse-proxy IPs across the roughly 6,200 exposed instances.

title: An update on residential proxies and the scraper situation
blurb: LWN's follow-up on the AI scraper problem reports that request floods now span millions of unique residential and mobile IPs, each hitting a site two or three times with fabricated user-agent strings before rotating away. The traffic comes from malware-infected home devices, increasingly media-streaming boxes, coordinated by command-and-control nodes, which makes IP-based blocking useless. Google's takedown of the IPIDEA proxy network cut LWN's scraper load for a few months, but the reprieve has already ended.

title: An analysis of 1M+ social media posts from April 24 to June 30: ~25% of longform posts with 250+ words were fully AI-generated; on LinkedIn, the figure was 41% (Max Spero/Pangram Labs)
blurb: New research from Pangram Labs analyzed over 1M social media posts between April 24 and June 30 and found that roughly 25% of longform posts with 250+ words were fully AI-generated, rising to 41% on LinkedIn. The finding raises the stakes for organic content strategy, since audiences and platform ranking systems are increasingly wading through AI-written posts to find the human voice that still drives engagement.

title: Google’s Universal Commerce Protocol: The SEO implications
blurb: Google introduced the Universal Commerce Protocol, an open-source standard co-developed with Shopify, Walmart, Target, and Etsy that lets AI agents discover, compare, and complete purchases inside AI Mode, Gemini, YouTube, and Gmail without a click to the merchant's site. For SEO teams, the success metric shifts from organic clicks and CTR to whether product data, Merchant Center feeds, and structured data are readable by agents that now transact on the buyer's behalf.

title: How to win SEO budget conversations with your CFO
blurb: CFOs approve SEO budgets based on pipeline risk and payback periods, not rankings, sessions, or organic traffic growth. The stronger case reframes SEO around cost-per-opportunity trends, CAC, and revenue exposure, the metrics a CFO already tracks. One enterprise software firm generated 274 demo requests in a month in 2026 versus 291 in 2008, despite spending roughly 8x more, and its head of search lost the budget review by leading with rankings instead of that structural cost problem.

title: Why frontloading your ad spend usually backfires
blurb: Frontloading paid media budgets before validating performance usually raises acquisition costs, slows algorithmic optimization, and erodes stakeholder confidence when early results miss. A phased rollout lets campaigns accumulate meaningful data, mature Quality Scores, and identify which audiences, keywords, and creative convert before you scale spend. Fire cheap "bullets" first to learn, then commit larger "cannonballs" once the numbers justify the confidence, reserving aggressive launches for the rare cases backed by strong historical data.

title: Google adds AI creation disclosure for ads
blurb: Google is adding AI-creation disclosures to ads, automatically tagging creatives made with its generative AI tools and adding an advertiser-facing control to flag AI use in ads built elsewhere. Detection runs on Google's SynthID watermarking plus C2PA metadata, and placement varies by local rules, so marketers using generative tools should expect a visible "how this ad was made" panel on their campaigns.

title: Gemini Intelligence signals a new era for search and commerce
blurb: Google announced Gemini Intelligence at the Android Show on May 12, a layer running beneath Android across laptops, phones, watches, and glasses that lets an AI agent read the screen and complete tasks without the user opening a webpage. The shift threatens the click-based SEO model, since agents transact on behalf of users, and one 2025 study found structured agent interactions cut processing 67.6% and costs 34% to 63% while holding 97.9% task success.

title: Farmer Boys Built a CTV Campaign Around One Creator’s Jingle. Salad Sales Rose 20%
blurb: Farmer Boys built its summer connected-TV commercial around a single creator's TikTok jingle about its Chopped Cobb Salad, skipping the film crew and studio shoot entirely, and salad sales rose 20% against the pre-campaign baseline. The team wrote CTV repurposing into the creator brief from the start, settling usage rights before filming and treating influencer content as a production pipeline for paid media rather than an organic-only channel. The lesson: designing for creator-to-CTV conversion upfront, not as an afterthought, is what makes the repurposing actually work.

title: Housing bill that includes a CBDC ban passed into law without Trump’s signature
blurb: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act became law without Trump's signature, and its provision barring the Federal Reserve from issuing a central bank digital currency now carries statutory force. The ban codifies an executive order from earlier in Trump's term, removing the Fed's authority to launch a retail CBDC absent new congressional approval.

title: Stablecoin issuer Circle just got the greenlight to operate as a bank. The shares are up 5%
blurb: Circle received an OCC national trust bank charter to operate as Circle National Trust, letting it hold and manage the cash and Treasury reserves backing USDC directly instead of relying on third-party banks and custodians. The charter puts Circle's roughly $73B stablecoin under a single federal regulator rather than state-by-state rules, as Coinbase, Ripple, Paxos, and others race for the same regulated footing; shares closed up about 5%.

title: U.S. government digital dollar set to be banned tonight under housing law's CBDC limit
blurb: A four-year ban on a US central bank digital currency becomes law tonight, tucked into a bipartisan housing-affordability bill that Trump declined to sign but which passes automatically after the 10-day constitutional window. The provision bars the Federal Reserve from issuing a digital dollar through 2030, a long-standing crypto-industry demand aimed at protecting private stablecoins from state competition.

title: Stablecoin firm Circle wins final OCC approval to open national trust bank
blurb: Circle received final OCC approval to open First National Digital Currency Bank, operating as Circle National Trust, a federally supervised national trust bank that will provide fiduciary digital asset custody for Circle and its affiliates at launch. Management of the USDC Reserve, the original goal of Circle's June 2025 application, is deferred to a later phase, and the charter bars the bank from accepting cash deposits or making loans.

title: Injective Labs GitHub Compromise Pushes Wallet-Key-Stealing npm Packages
blurb: Attackers compromised the Injective Labs SDK GitHub repository and published a malicious @injectivelabs/sdk-ts@1.20.21 to npm, embedding fake telemetry that exfiltrated wallet private keys and mnemonic seed phrases. The threat actor pinned the poisoned version across 17 additional @injectivelabs packages, exposing transitive dependents, and the compromised artifacts remained downloadable on GitHub even after the npm release was deprecated.

title: Circle soars after securing U.S. trust bank approval in crypto expansion
blurb: Circle received final approval from the U.S. Office of the Comptroller of the Currency to establish Circle National Trust, a federally supervised national trust bank offering fiduciary custody for digital assets. Shares of the USDC issuer rose 14% in pre-market trading, and the charter positions Circle to eventually manage its stablecoin reserves under OCC supervision alongside peers like Kraken and Crypto.com pursuing federal licenses.

title: Cambridge research puts 31% of Ethereum node activity in the US, where a third offline can stall finalization
blurb: Cambridge's Centre for Alternative Finance mapped Ethereum node hosting and found 31% of node activity in the US and roughly 39% across the EU excluding the UK, with nodes clustered on Hetzner, AWS, and OVH. The report notes that Ethereum stops finalizing checkpoints once more than a third of validators go offline at once, making that provider concentration a live network risk. Node location also carries legal weight, since the SEC cited US-hosted nodes to claim jurisdiction over Ethereum in 2022.

title: Gauntlet Raises $125M Series C From SBI Holdings
blurb: Gauntlet, a DeFi risk management and vault curation firm with $1.42B in assets under advisement, raised a $125M Series C led solely by Japanese conglomerate SBI Holdings. The firm, last valued at $1B in 2022, will use the capital to expand stablecoin coverage and build tooling for institutions moving capital onchain across Base, BNB Chain, and Ethereum.

title: Polymarket Applies for US License to Offer Margin Trading
blurb: Polymarket applied for a US futures commission merchant license on July 3, filing with the National Futures Association to offer margin trading that lets users open positions without posting full collateral upfront. The move follows rival Kalshi, whose FCM license and subsequent perpetual contracts generated more than $5.5B in volume within two weeks, and still requires CFTC approval to amend Polymarket's rulebook.

title: Laser Attack Resets Tangem Wallet Passwords on Cards That Can't Be Patched
blurb: Ledger's Donjon security team showed that a precisely timed laser pulse aimed at the Samsung secure element inside a Tangem wallet card can force the chip into recovery mode and reset the card password without the old password or a backup card, handing full control of the funds to the attacker. The attack needs physical possession, a ~$250,000 lab, and visible destructive decapping, and Tangem cards can't be patched, so every unit shipped carries the flaw permanently.

title: Uniswap Floats Turning On Protocol Fees for v4 Pools
blurb: Uniswap Labs proposed activating protocol fees on a subset of Uniswap v4 pools, extending the UNIfication fee rollout that already burns UNI across all v2 and v3 pools on 11 chains. A five-day Snapshot temperature check runs July 7-12 ahead of onchain voting the week of July 13, with fees routed through a new two-contract V4 Fee Controller built for v4's per-block, unlimited-tier hook design.

title: Here’s what’s next for the Clarity Act as Congress returns to Washington
blurb: The Senate is expected to release combined text of the Clarity Act crypto market-structure bill as soon as next week, with the House version passed nearly a year ago and just July plus the first week of August to reach a floor vote before recess. Unresolved sticking points include a safe-harbor provision shielding non-custodial developers from money-transmitter rules and whether to add ethics restrictions.

title: Kraken is rebuilding its app around agentic trading as crypto exchanges evolve beyond crypto
blurb: Kraken is rebuilding its app around agentic trading, giving users AI agents that continuously monitor markets, surface opportunities, and draft portfolios based on stated goals and risk tolerance, with trades still requiring human approval. The launch positions AI-native tooling as the next competitive front for crypto exchanges, extending automated execution beyond the fixed-rule bots that active traders already run.

title: Hyundai becomes first major South Korean company to introduce internal stablecoin transfers
blurb: Hyundai became the first major South Korean company to move a stablecoin-based cross-border treasury system into production, running its first live transfer of $20,000 from its US unit to its Mexico unit via USDT on Avalanche. The settlement took about seven minutes versus the three to four hours of traditional banking rails, and Hyundai plans to expand to more corridors and currencies, including a Europe pilot with Circle and Visa.

title: Kraken to relaunch its mobile app with agentic trading and advice at the center
blurb: Kraken is rebuilding its mobile app around agentic trading, adding autonomous AI agents that monitor markets, suggest trades against a user's stated goals, and learn from outcomes rather than functioning as a bolted-on assistant. The move follows Gemini's April API opening and Coinbase's SEC-registered Advisor, with investment advice split across Payward Interactive for crypto and the registered Kraken Adviser for securities.

title: Sky Reports Record $419M Revenue Run-Rate for June 2026
blurb: Sky, the lending and stablecoin protocol formerly known as MakerDAO, reported a record $419M annualized gross revenue run-rate in its June 2026 update, alongside $6.12B in TVL and cumulative sUSDS savings-rate distributions past $250M since inception. DefiLlama's fee tracking puts the annualized pace near $367M, roughly in line with Sky's self-reported figure.

title: South Korea Super-App Toss to Run Won Stablecoin Pilot on OP Stack
blurb: Toss, the South Korean fintech app with roughly 30 million users, began a three-month proof of concept for a Korean won stablecoin built on Optimism's OP Stack, alongside Sunnyside Labs' Privacy Boost tool for shielding transaction data while preserving compliance access. The pilot tests whether a regulated institution can handle settlement, KYC, and AML on a public chain, and marks the fourth OP Stack adoption by a financial institution in a year.

title: Ledger researchers disclose Tangem card flaw; Tangem says risk to everyday users is ‘virtually non-existent’
blurb: Ledger Donjon researchers disclosed a laser fault injection attack against Tangem hardware wallet cards that bypasses a firmware recovery-state check to reset the card password and take control of the wallet. The exploit needs physical possession of the card plus roughly $250,000 in lab equipment and about two hours per card, and it cannot be patched because Tangem cards lack a firmware update mechanism. The practical risk is limited to a lost or stolen card, since the attack is invasive and cannot be performed covertly.

title: Empery Digital offloads 1,400 bitcoins to help fund AI pivot, pay debt
blurb: Empery Digital sold 1,400 bitcoins for roughly $87.1 million over the past two months, its largest treasury reduction to date, using the proceeds to repay $10 million in debt and fund a $65 million commitment to a US AI data center project. The Nasdaq-listed firm, formerly powersports maker Volcon, still holds 1,514 BTC but trades down about 82% since launching its bitcoin treasury strategy last July.

title: DOJ to drop charges against man accused in $722 million BitClub crypto Ponzi scheme: Bloomberg
blurb: The DOJ plans to dismiss with prejudice its case against Matthew Goettsche, alleged ringleader of the $722 million BitClub Network crypto Ponzi scheme, reversing a 2019 indictment that was nearing trial. Three co-defendants have already pleaded guilty, and Bloomberg reports Goettsche assembled a legal team with Trump-administration connections to lobby the department for relief.

title: Binance co-CEO says 70% of EU withdrawals went to self-custody after MiCA deadline, with just 30% going to licensed platforms
blurb: Binance co-CEO Richard Teng said 70% of the funds European users withdrew after the exchange suspended EU services moved to self-custodied wallets, with the remaining 30% going to MiCA-regulated platforms. Teng argued the split undermines MiCA's risk-reduction goal, since self-hosted assets fall outside the AML and KYC controls that apply to licensed exchanges.

title: ‘Communication challenge, nothing more’: Standard Chartered keeps $100,000 bitcoin target, calls Strategy selloff a signaling problem
blurb: Standard Chartered maintained its end-2026 bitcoin price target of $100,000, framing recent weakness tied to Strategy Inc. as a communication problem rather than a balance-sheet one. The bank's Geoffrey Kendrick pointed to Strategy's pivot from "never sell bitcoin" toward using its 843,775 BTC as collateral for STRC, a perpetual preferred stock with roughly $10B notional that hit an intraday low of $71.25 against $100 par on June 26.

title: Trump admin eases export controls for UAE; Warren blasts 'corrupt' provision
blurb: The Commerce Department moved to ease export controls on the United Arab Emirates, pledging "favorable review" of license applications for MGX, the UAE-backed firm that used Trump-family stablecoin USD1 to fund its $2B investment in Binance. Senator Elizabeth Warren called the provision "corrupt," citing a reported $263M Trump windfall tied to the deal and G42's newly granted license-free access to advanced AI chips.

title: New Hampshire’s $100 million bitcoin bond proposal fails to pass final vote
blurb: New Hampshire's Executive Council voted 3-2 to reject a proposal for up to $100 million in bitcoin-collateralized conduit revenue bonds tied to CleanSpark, which would have deposited roughly $160 million in BTC as collateral with BitGo as custodian. The rejection kills a deal Governor Kelly Ayotte had backed and that Moody's rated Ba2, two notches below investment grade, ending one of the first state-level attempts to structure a bitcoin-backed bond.

title: Money launderer accused of stealing seized crypto while in prison
blurb: Rossen Iossifov, a Bulgarian national already serving 121 months for laundering nearly $5M for the Alexandria Online Auction Fraud Network, was charged with moving $290,000 in government-seized cryptocurrency out of a forfeited account while in prison. Prosecutors say he conspired in January 2024 to route the funds through multiple exchanges and mixing services, and he now faces up to 25 additional years.

title: Stablecoin firm Circle wins final OCC approval to open national trust bank
blurb: Circle received final OCC approval to open First National Digital Currency Bank, operating as Circle National Trust, a federally supervised national trust bank offering fiduciary digital asset custody. Management of the USDC Reserve, the original goal of Circle's June 2025 application, is deferred to a future phase, and the charter bars the bank from taking cash deposits or making loans.

title: Housing bill that includes a CBDC ban passed into law without Trump’s signature
blurb: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act became law without Trump's signature, and it carries a provision permanently barring the Federal Reserve from issuing a central bank digital currency. The CBDC ban forecloses a US retail digital dollar as a settlement rail, ceding that ground to bank-issued stablecoins and existing instant-payment systems like FedNow and RTP.

title: Stablecoin issuer Circle just got the greenlight to operate as a bank. The shares are up 5%
blurb: The OCC granted Circle a national trust-bank charter under the name Circle National Trust, letting the stablecoin issuer manage USDC reserves directly instead of relying on third-party banks and custodians for the roughly $73B backing the token. The charter, required for large issuers under the GENIUS Act, swaps 50-state oversight for a single federal regulator and follows OCC moves involving Coinbase, Ripple, BitGo, and Paxos.

title: Circle soars after securing U.S. trust bank approval in crypto expansion
blurb: Circle received final approval from the OCC to establish Circle National Trust, a federally supervised national trust bank that will provide fiduciary digital asset custody for Circle and its affiliates, with shares rising 14% pre-market. The charter also positions Circle to eventually manage USDC's reserves under OCC supervision, and it adds to a run of crypto firms including Crypto.com, BitGo, and Paxos securing federal banking approvals.

title: U.S. government digital dollar set to be banned tonight under housing law's CBDC limit
blurb: A four-year ban on a U.S. central bank digital currency becomes law at midnight Saturday, riding along on the bipartisan housing-affordability bill that President Trump declined to sign but that passes automatically under the Constitution's 10-day window. The provision bars the Federal Reserve from issuing a digital dollar through 2030, a long-standing crypto-industry demand that removes any prospect of a government-run competitor to private stablecoins.

title: Gauntlet Raises $125M Series C From SBI Holdings
blurb: Gauntlet, a DeFi risk-management and vault-curation firm with $1.42B in assets under advisement, raised a $125M Series C with SBI Holdings as sole investor, its first round since a 2022 Series B that valued it at $1B. The capital funds expansion into traditional capital markets, wider stablecoin coverage, and new onchain products, positioning Gauntlet as quantitative guardrails for institutions moving capital onchain.

title: Here’s what’s next for the Clarity Act as Congress returns to Washington
blurb: The Senate returns from its Fourth of July break with a few weeks to move the Clarity Act, the crypto market-structure bill, to the floor before the August recess and midterm campaigning take over. Passage still hinges on unresolved provisions, including a safe harbor exempting non-custodial developers from money-transmitter rules, which the industry backs but some law enforcement groups and Catholic leaders say could weaken anti-trafficking safeguards.

title: Options for Everyone
blurb: The National Stock Exchange of India became the world's busiest equity derivatives market on the back of retail traders who mostly lose money, with SEBI finding 91% of participants unprofitable and 9.6 million individuals losing a combined $11 billion in the year to March 2025. The piece traces the pipeline feeding that volume, from trading academies like Avadhut Sathe's, which collected $63 million in course fees while two-thirds of graduates lost money over the following six months.

title: JPMorgan Builds AI Agents That Beat 60/40 Model in Backtests
blurb: JPMorgan built a system of AI agents that allocate capital directly and beat a standard 60/40 portfolio in backtests, moving the bank's AI work from stock-picking and risk tools toward autonomous asset allocation. Backtested outperformance is not live performance, but a major bank testing agents that move money themselves signals where institutional portfolio management is heading.

title: Polymarket Applies for US License to Offer Margin Trading
blurb: Polymarket applied for a US futures commission merchant license through affiliate Coming Home GBA LLC, seeking to offer margin trading that would let users open positions without posting full collateral. The move requires CFTC approval to amend its rulebook and follows rival Kalshi, which secured its own FCM license this year and drew over $5.5B in perpetuals volume within two weeks.

title: Kraken is rebuilding its app around agentic trading as crypto exchanges evolve beyond crypto
blurb: Kraken is rebuilding its app around agentic trading, giving retail users AI agents that continuously monitor markets, surface opportunities, and draft portfolios based on stated goals and risk tolerance. The system is agentic but keeps a human in the loop for execution, reflecting a broader push toward AI-native trading products as exchanges compete beyond spot crypto.

title: Kraken to relaunch its mobile app with agentic trading and advice at the center
blurb: Kraken is relaunching its mobile app around agentic trading, with autonomous AI agents that monitor markets, suggest trades against user-defined goals, and learn from outcomes rather than functioning as a bolted-on assistant. The move follows Gemini opening its APIs to agentic setups and Coinbase's SEC-registered Advisor robo-advisor, with advice split between Payward Interactive for crypto and SEC-registered Kraken Adviser LLC for securities.

title: Hyundai becomes first major South Korean company to introduce internal stablecoin transfers
blurb: Hyundai became the first major South Korean company to move a stablecoin-based cross-border treasury system into production, using USDT on Avalanche to send $20,000 from its U.S. unit to its Mexico unit in about seven minutes instead of the usual three to four hours. The carmaker plans to expand to more corridors and currencies, including a European pilot testing local-currency transfers and FX costs with Circle and Visa.

title: South Korea Super-App Toss to Run Won Stablecoin Pilot on OP Stack
blurb: Toss, South Korea's fintech super-app with roughly 30 million users, launched a three-month pilot testing a Korean won stablecoin on Optimism's OP Stack, with Sunnyside Labs providing a privacy tool that shields transaction data while preserving compliance access. The proof of concept tests whether a regulated institution can handle settlement, KYC/AML, and transaction privacy on a public chain, though a live KRW stablecoin hinges on South Korea's still-unfinished stablecoin licensing framework.

title: Trump admin eases export controls for UAE; Warren blasts 'corrupt' provision
blurb: The Commerce Department will "favorably review" export license applications for MGX, the UAE-backed firm that used the Trump family-linked USD1 stablecoin to fund its $2B Binance investment, as part of a rule upgrading UAE access to advanced AI chips. Senator Elizabeth Warren called the provision "corrupt," citing Trump's reported $263M windfall tied to the deal and national-security concerns over chip diversion to China.

title: Kazakh Tycoon Turlov’s Freedom Raises $300 Million for Expansion
blurb: Freedom Holding Corp., the brokerage controlled by Kazakh billionaire Timur Turlov, raised $300M in a share sale to fund its ongoing international expansion. The capital gives the Nasdaq-listed group more room to push its brokerage, banking, and insurance operations beyond Central Asia into markets across Europe, the Middle East, and the US.

title: OpenPayd CEO on sovereignty, stablecoins and why the UK must move faster, as the $1.1B fintech heads for Nasdaq
blurb: OpenPayd, the B2B infrastructure provider that lets businesses offer fiat and stablecoin payment services without holding their own financial license, is going public on Nasdaq via a SPAC merger at a $1.14B valuation. CEO Iana Dimitrova frames payments infrastructure as a matter of national sovereignty and security, and argues the UK has a time-sensitive window to convert its fintech strengths into lasting global leadership.
